name: AF - anticoagulation (stroke prevention)
presentation: AF diagnosed — asymptomatic or symptomatic; stroke risk assessment required at diagnosis
Previous TIA or stroke — highest risk group, anticoagulation clearly indicated
signs: Neurological signs of prior embolic stroke — focal deficit, dysphasia
No signs specific to anticoagulation need — risk is determined by scoring, not examination

bedside: ECG: confirm AF (absent P waves, irregular R-R) and not sinus rhythm with ectopics
BP: hypertension is a stroke risk factor (H in CHA2DS2-VASc) AND a bleeding risk factor (H in HAS-BLED)
bloods: U&E and eGFR: renal function required to select and dose DOAC (apixaban/rivaroxaban dose-reduced in CKD); contraindication in severe renal failure
LFTs: hepatic impairment affects DOAC metabolism; warfarin requires INR monitoring
FBC: anaemia or thrombocytopenia as bleeding risk factors before anticoagulation
imaging: Echo: valvular AF (mitral stenosis, mechanical valve) — mandates warfarin not DOAC; assess for LA thrombus

mx: CHA2DS2-VASc score — anticoagulate if score ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women (score ≥1/≥2 consider); do not anticoagulate score 0/1 in men
DOAC first-line (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) — superior to warfarin for non-valvular AF; no routine INR monitoring needed
Warfarin — use ONLY for valvular AF (mitral stenosis, mechanical heart valve) where DOACs are contraindicated
traps: Aspirin is NOT an acceptable alternative to anticoagulation for stroke prevention in AF — provides minimal benefit with similar bleeding risk; NICE explicitly recommends against antiplatelet monotherapy for AF stroke prevention
Paroxysmal AF carries the same stroke risk as persistent/permanent AF — CHA2DS2-VASc and anticoagulation decision applies equally

name: Acute coronary syndrome (STEMI)
presentation: Central crushing chest pain radiating to jaw/left arm, onset at rest, >20 min duration
Diaphoresis, nausea, vomiting — autonomic features distinguishing from stable angina
Sudden dyspnoea if large territory infarct causes acute LV dysfunction
Silent presentation in diabetics and elderly — present with syncope, acute HF, or confusion only
signs: Tachycardia (compensatory) or bradycardia (inferior STEMI with vagal activation)
S3 gallop if significant LV impairment
New pansystolic murmur — papillary muscle rupture (MR) or VSD as mechanical complication
Hypotension with raised JVP in right ventricular infarction (inferior STEMI + RV involvement)

bedside: ECG: ST elevation ≥1mm in ≥2 contiguous limb leads or ≥2mm in precordial leads — anterior STEMI in V1-V4, inferior in II/III/aVF, lateral in I/aVL/V5-V6
ECG: reciprocal ST depression in leads opposite to infarct territory (e.g. inferior STEMI → ST depression V1-V3)
ECG: new LBBB in context of chest pain — treat as STEMI equivalent
Obs: BP both arms to exclude aortic dissection before thrombolysis/PPCI
bloods: Troponin I/T: rises 3-6h post-onset, peaks 12-24h, remains elevated 7-14d — use serial troponins at 0h and 3h
FBC: baseline; raised WCC from sterile infarct inflammation
U&E: baseline renal function before contrast for angiography
Lipid profile: total cholesterol, LDL — fasting sample, guides statin therapy
imaging: CXR: pulmonary oedema (upper lobe diversion, interstitial shadowing) if large anterior STEMI with LV failure; normal CXR does not exclude STEMI
Echo (post-reperfusion): regional wall motion abnormality in affected territory, assess LVEF to guide prognosis and therapy

mx: Reperfusion — primary PCI within 120 min of first medical contact (preferred); thrombolysis if PCI unavailable within 120 min
Dual antiplatelet — aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (e.g. ticagrelor) to prevent stent thrombosis and re-infarction
Anticoagulation — unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin as adjunct to PCI
traps: Posterior STEMI: no ST elevation — look for ST depression V1-V3 with tall R waves (mirror image); confirm with posterior leads V7-V9
Right ventricular infarction complicating inferior STEMI: hypotension responds to IV fluids NOT diuretics — giving nitrates/diuretics can precipitate profound hypotension

name: Acute heart failure / pulmonary oedema
presentation: Sudden onset severe dyspnoea at rest, often nocturnal — patient woken from sleep
Orthopnoea — cannot lie flat; prefers sitting upright or leaning forward
Pink frothy sputum — alveolar oedema with haemorrhagic tinge
Profound anxiety, sensation of drowning
signs: Severe respiratory distress: tachypnoea, use of accessory muscles, unable to complete sentences
Bibasal coarse crackles extending to mid-zones or upper zones — alveolar flooding
S3 gallop and tachycardia — hyperdynamic circulation or failing ventricle
Cold peripheries, mottled skin — low output state (cardiogenic shock if BP also low)
Raised JVP, bilateral pitting oedema — fluid overload

bedside: ECG: identify precipitant — AF with rapid ventricular rate, acute STEMI/NSTEMI, new LBBB, tachyarrhythmia
SpO2: hypoxia confirms diagnosis; target SpO2 ≥94%
BM: exclude hypoglycaemia as precipitant/comorbidity
bloods: BNP/NT-proBNP: markedly elevated — confirms cardiac cause; useful when diagnosis uncertain vs respiratory cause of dyspnoea
Troponin: exclude ACS as precipitant of acute HF
U&E: baseline before diuresis; avoid over-diuresis causing AKI
ABG: type 1 respiratory failure (low PaO2, normal/low PaCO2); type 2 if exhausted
imaging: CXR (portable/supine): cardiomegaly, bilateral perihilar bat-wing shadowing, upper lobe blood diversion, Kerley B lines at costophrenic angles, pleural effusions (blunted costophrenic angles)

mx: IV loop diuretic (furosemide) — promotes rapid fluid removal; reduces preload and pulmonary venous congestion
Oxygen — high-flow if SpO2 <94%; NIV (CPAP) if type 1 respiratory failure persisting on oxygen — reduces need for intubation
Treat precipitant — cardiovert AF, reperfuse STEMI, treat hypertensive crisis with vasodilators (GTN infusion)
traps: Acute pulmonary oedema vs COPD exacerbation can both present with severe dyspnoea and crackles/wheeze — BNP and ECG are key differentiators; NIV is used in both but CPAP (no backup rate) preferred in pulmonary oedema, BiPAP in COPD
Avoid high-flow oxygen in patients with known COPD and CO2 retention — target SpO2 88-92% to avoid suppressing hypoxic drive

name: Atrial fibrillation - rate/rhythm
presentation: Palpitations — irregular, fast, may be episodic if paroxysmal AF
Dyspnoea, reduced exercise tolerance from loss of atrial kick reducing cardiac output
Fatigue, presyncope — especially with rapid ventricular rate
Incidental finding on pulse check — lone AF may be asymptomatic
signs: Irregularly irregular pulse — hallmark; pulse deficit (apex faster than radial) with rapid AF
Tachycardia (ventricular rate typically 100-180 bpm if uncontrolled)
Signs of haemodynamic compromise: hypotension, pulmonary oedema — indicates urgent cardioversion

bedside: ECG: absent P waves, irregularly irregular R-R intervals, fibrillatory baseline (best seen V1), narrow QRS unless aberrant conduction or pre-excitation (WPW)
ECG (WPW warning): broad irregular complexes at very rapid rate — delta waves, do NOT use AV-nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, adenosine) as they precipitate VF via accessory pathway
bloods: TFTs: thyrotoxicosis is a reversible cause — TSH suppressed, raised fT4/fT3
FBC: anaemia as precipitant; infection causing AF exacerbation
U&E: electrolytes (hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia increase ectopy)
LFTs and alcohol markers: alcohol excess is major AF trigger
imaging: Echo: LA size (dilated LA predicts recurrence after cardioversion), LV function, valvular disease (mitral stenosis → AF), exclude intracardiac thrombus before cardioversion

mx: Rate control (chronic AF or onset >48h) — beta-blocker or rate-limiting calcium channel blocker (diltiazem/verapamil) to target resting rate <110 bpm

Rhythm control (symptomatic or onset <48h with haemodynamic stability) — DC cardioversion or pharmacological cardioversion (flecainide if no structural heart disease, amiodarone if structural disease)
Haemodynamic instability → immediate synchronised DC cardioversion regardless of anticoagulation status
traps: AF with WPW: broad complex rapid irregular rhythm — AV-nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, beta-blockers) are contraindicated; accelerate conduction via accessory pathway → VF
Cardioversion after >48h AF without anticoagulation risks thromboembolism — must anticoagulate ≥3 weeks before or perform TOE to exclude LA thrombus first

name: Bradycardia / when to pace
presentation: Syncope or pre-syncope — sudden loss of cardiac output during complete AV block or sinus arrest
Dizziness, lightheadedness on standing or exertion
Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance — chronotropic incompetence
Palpitations from escape beats; patient may notice slow regular or irregular pulse
signs: Bradycardia <60 bpm on pulse — confirm on ECG as rate and regularity
Cannon A waves in JVP — complete heart block with dissociated atrial and ventricular contraction
Variable S1 intensity — complete heart block (PR interval varies)
Hypotension if haemodynamically compromising bradycardia

bedside: ECG: sinus bradycardia (P before every QRS, rate <60), 1st degree AV block (PR >200ms), 2nd degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach — PR lengthens then P dropped), 2nd degree Mobitz II (fixed PR, sudden P dropped — unstable, may progress to complete block), complete (3rd degree) AV block (P and QRS dissociated, escape rhythm <40 bpm)
ECG: pause identification — sinus arrest, SA exit block
bloods: U&E: hyperkalaemia causes bradycardia, AV block, and widened QRS — K+ >6.5 with ECG changes is emergency
TFTs: hypothyroidism causes sinus bradycardia and conduction disease
Drug levels/history: beta-blockers, digoxin toxicity, rate-limiting CCBs as reversible causes
imaging: Echo: structural heart disease (aortic stenosis, cardiomyopathy) as substrate for conduction disease; assess LV function before pacemaker decision

mx: Atropine IV — first-line for symptomatic bradycardia (increases SA node rate and AV conduction); ineffective in complete heart block with ventricular escape (no vagal tone at this level)
Temporary transvenous pacing — for haemodynamically compromising bradycardia not responding to atropine; bridge to permanent pacemaker
Permanent pacemaker — indicated for complete heart block, symptomatic Mobitz II, sinus node disease with symptoms; dual-chamber (DDD) maintains AV synchrony
traps: Mobitz II is far more dangerous than Mobitz I (Wenckebach) — Wenckebach is often benign and vagally mediated; Mobitz II indicates infranodal disease and can progress unpredictably to complete heart block requiring permanent pacemaker
Atropine is ineffective and may worsen bradycardia in heart transplant patients (no vagal innervation) — use isoprenaline or pacing

name: Chronic heart failure (HFrEF)
presentation: Progressive exertional dyspnoea — NYHA classification (I: no symptoms at normal activity → IV: symptoms at rest)
Orthopnoea — number of pillows needed; paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea
Ankle swelling, worse by evening — dependent oedema from venous hypertension
Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance — low cardiac output and skeletal muscle deconditioning
signs: Raised JVP — elevated venous pressure from fluid overload
Bibasal fine crackles — pulmonary oedema (may be absent in chronic compensated HF)
Pitting oedema bilateral to sacrum if bed-bound
S3 gallop (ventricular filling sound) — volume overloaded dilated ventricle
Displaced apex beat (lateral and inferiorly) — cardiomegaly from dilated LV

bedside: ECG: LVH, LBBB (dyssynchrony — indicates CRT candidacy), AF (common precipitant/complication), Q waves (ischaemic aetiology)
Obs: SpO2 — oxygen saturations, resting tachycardia as compensatory mechanism
bloods: BNP or NT-proBNP: elevated proportional to degree of LV dysfunction — NT-proBNP >400 pg/mL supports diagnosis; excellent negative predictive value if normal
U&E: renal function (cardiorenal syndrome); hyponatraemia suggests severe failure and poor prognosis
TFTs: thyroid disease as reversible cause
FBC: anaemia worsens symptoms and is a treatment target
imaging: Echo: LVEF <40% defines HFrEF; dilated LV, regional wall motion abnormality (ischaemic) vs global (dilated cardiomyopathy); mitral regurgitation (functional)
CXR: cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio >0.5), upper lobe blood diversion, Kerley B lines (interstitial oedema), bilateral perihilar haze — 'bat wing' appearance in acute decompensation

mx: ACE inhibitor (or ARB if intolerant) — reduces afterload and neurohormonal activation; improves mortality in HFrEF
Beta-blocker (bisoprolol/carvedilol) — reduces sympathetic drive; improves mortality; start low, uptitrate when clinically stable
Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (spironolactone/eplerenone) — add if symptoms persist despite ACEi + BB; monitor K+ and renal function
traps: SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) now have evidence in HFrEF regardless of diabetes status — increasingly included as fourth pillar of HF therapy (Year 4 level awareness)
ACE inhibitor should not be started during acute decompensation — wait until euvolaemic and haemodynamically stable before introducing/uptitrating

name: Hypertension
presentation: Usually asymptomatic — diagnosed incidentally on routine BP measurement
Headache (occipital, morning) — more common in severe/malignant hypertension
Epistaxis — associated but not specific
Hypertensive emergency: severe headache, visual changes, confusion, chest pain, focal neurology
signs: BP ≥140/90 mmHg on two separate readings (clinic BP) — confirm with ABPM or home monitoring
Fundoscopy: AV nipping, silver wiring (chronic hypertensive changes); flame haemorrhages, papilloedema (grade III/IV — hypertensive emergency)
Signs of end-organ damage: LVH on ECG/echo, displaced apex beat, renal bruits (renovascular cause)

bedside: ECG: LVH (Sokolow-Lyon: S in V1 + R in V5/V6 >35mm) — indicates end-organ damage, raises cardiovascular risk classification
Urine dip: proteinuria (renal damage), haematuria (renal parenchymal disease or renal artery stenosis as secondary cause)
bloods: U&E: renal function (hypertensive nephropathy, renovascular cause — raised creatinine); hypokalaemia in primary hyperaldosteronism (Conn's syndrome)
Fasting glucose and HbA1c: diabetes as major cardiovascular risk modifier
Lipid profile: total cholesterol, LDL — cardiovascular risk calculation (QRISK)
TFTs: hyperthyroidism as secondary cause of hypertension
imaging: Echo: LV mass (hypertrophy), diastolic dysfunction — more sensitive than ECG for LVH; also guides treatment if HF coexists
Renal USS: asymmetric kidneys (renovascular hypertension or chronic pyelonephritis), adult polycystic kidney disease

mx: Lifestyle — DASH diet, salt restriction, weight loss, aerobic exercise, alcohol reduction; first-line for stage 1 HTN with low CV risk
Antihypertensive drugs — step approach: <55 years start ACE inhibitor (or ARB); ≥55 years or Black ethnicity start calcium channel blocker; add diuretic (thiazide-like) as step 2
Target BP <140/90 mmHg (clinic) in most; <130/80 mmHg if established CVD, diabetes, or CKD with proteinuria
traps: Hypertensive emergency (BP >180/120 + end-organ damage: papilloedema, encephalopathy, AKI) — do NOT lower BP rapidly; gradual reduction over 24-48h to avoid watershed ischaemia and stroke
White-coat hypertension is common — always confirm with ABPM (ambulatory blood pressure monitoring) or home readings before diagnosing and treating

name: NSTEMI / unstable angina
presentation: Chest pain at rest or on minimal exertion, lasting >10 min, not fully relieved by GTN
New-onset angina (within 4 weeks) or crescendo pattern — increasing frequency/severity/duration
Dyspnoea, fatigue as anginal equivalents, especially in women and diabetics
Epigastric pain with nausea — inferior territory ischaemia mimicking GI pathology
signs: Often no abnormal signs at rest — examination used to identify complications and rule out differentials
Transient S3 or new MR murmur during ischaemic episode
Signs of heart failure if large territory or haemodynamic compromise

bedside: ECG: ST depression (horizontal or downsloping ≥0.5mm) or T-wave inversion in ischaemic territory — does NOT show ST elevation
ECG: may be entirely normal — a normal ECG does not exclude NSTEMI; repeat during pain
bloods: High-sensitivity troponin I/T: detectable within 1-2h; serial 0h and 1h or 0h and 3h protocol; elevation distinguishes NSTEMI from unstable angina (UA has no troponin rise)
FBC: anaemia as precipitant of demand ischaemia
U&E and eGFR: renal function before anticoagulation and contrast
BNP/NT-proBNP: elevated if ischaemia has caused LV dysfunction — prognostic value
imaging: CXR: exclude pulmonary oedema and alternative diagnoses (pneumothorax, aortic widening)
Echo: regional wall motion abnormality if significant ischaemia; baseline LVEF for risk stratification

mx: Dual antiplatelet — aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor/clopidogrel) immediately; reduces re-infarction risk
Anticoagulation — LMWH or fondaparinux for anti-thrombotic effect while awaiting risk stratification
Risk stratification with GRACE score — high-risk (score >140) → early invasive strategy (coronary angiography within 72h)
traps: Normal ECG at rest is common in NSTEMI — must repeat during pain; diagnosis rests on troponin kinetics not ECG
Unstable angina and NSTEMI are managed identically initially — the distinction is troponin rise (NSTEMI) vs no rise (UA)

name: Acute COPD exacerbation
presentation: Acute worsening dyspnoea beyond normal day-to-day variation
Increased sputum volume and/or purulence — suggests infective trigger (commonest: Haemophilus influenzae, Streptococcus pneumoniae, Moraxella catarrhalis)
Cough worsening — may produce green/yellow sputum
Wheeze, confusion (CO2 retention) in severe exacerbation
signs: Tachypnoea, tachycardia — markers of severity
Use of accessory muscles, paradoxical breathing — impending respiratory failure
Central cyanosis — severe hypoxaemia
Asterixis (CO2 retention flap) — confirms hypercapnic respiratory failure
Right heart failure signs: raised JVP, peripheral oedema — cor pulmonale exacerbated

bedside: SpO2: target 88-92% in COPD exacerbation to avoid suppressing hypoxic drive; NOT 94-98%
Peak flow: less useful than spirometry in COPD but may guide severity assessment
bloods: ABG: essential — PaO2 <8kPa (hypoxaemia), raised PaCO2 (hypercapnia), low pH (acidosis) in type 2 failure; pH <7.26 or worsening on controlled oxygen → NIV
FBC: raised WBC and CRP suggest bacterial infection — guides antibiotic decision
U&E: theophylline toxicity risk if used; hypokalaemia from bronchodilators
Blood cultures: if pyrexial — gram-negative bacteria particularly important in severe exacerbation
imaging: CXR: exclude pneumothorax (in emphysematous bullous disease), consolidation (infective exacerbation/pneumonia), pneumomediastinum; assess cardiac size for cor pulmonale

mx: Controlled oxygen (24-28% Venturi) — target SpO2 88-92%; avoid high-flow oxygen causing CO2 retention and respiratory acidosis
Short-acting bronchodilators — salbutamol + ipratropium nebulisers; driven by air (not high-flow O2) unless severe hypoxia
NIV (BiPAP) — for type 2 respiratory failure (pH <7.35, PaCO2 >6kPa) not responding to medical therapy; reduces intubation rate and mortality
traps: High-flow oxygen in COPD with CO2 retention worsens hypercapnia — mechanism: Haldane effect (O2 displaces CO2 from haemoglobin), loss of hypoxic drive, V/Q mismatch worsening; always use controlled oxygen
Not all COPD exacerbations need antibiotics — purulent sputum or consolidation on CXR is the indication; viral exacerbations (commonest trigger) do not benefit from antibiotics

name: Acute asthma exacerbation
presentation: Sudden worsening dyspnoea and wheeze not responding to repeated SABA doses
Unable to complete sentences (moderate-severe), unable to speak (life-threatening)
Cough worse at night; accessory muscle use
Precipitants: URTI, allergen exposure, stopping ICS, beta-blocker prescription
signs: Moderate: PEFR 50-75% of best/predicted, SpO2 ≥92%, able to talk
Severe: PEFR 33-50%, RR ≥25/min, HR ≥110 bpm, unable to complete sentences
Life-threatening: PEFR <33%, SpO2 <92%, silent chest (no wheeze = no air entry), cyanosis, bradycardia, confusion, exhaustion
Silent chest is an ominous sign — severe obstruction with no airflow, not improvement

bedside: Peak expiratory flow (PEF): % of personal best or predicted — defines severity; repeat after bronchodilator to assess response
SpO2: <92% indicates severe/life-threatening; target ≥94-98%
ECG: sinus tachycardia expected; exclude arrhythmia if very tachycardic
bloods: ABG: initially low PaCO2 (hyperventilation); normal or rising PaCO2 in life-threatening attack indicates fatigue and impending respiratory arrest — even 'normal' CO2 in acute asthma is alarming
U&E: hypokalaemia from repeated salbutamol nebulisers (beta-2 shifts K+ intracellularly) — monitor and replace
imaging: CXR: exclude pneumothorax (life-threatening complication of severe asthma), lobar collapse from mucus plugging, consolidation as precipitant

mx: High-dose SABA (salbutamol) nebulisers back-to-back — bronchodilation; driven by oxygen in severe/life-threatening
Systemic corticosteroids (oral prednisolone or IV hydrocortisone) — reduces airway inflammation; onset 4-6h; give early in all moderate/severe attacks
Ipratropium bromide nebulisers — add to salbutamol in moderate-severe attack; additive bronchodilation via anticholinergic mechanism
traps: Normal PaCO2 in acute asthma is a red flag — patient should be hypocapnic from hyperventilation; normalising CO2 means tiring and approaching respiratory failure; prepare for ICU
Do NOT prescribe beta-blockers to asthmatic patients — even 'cardioselective' beta-blockers can precipitate fatal bronchospasm

name: Asthma - chronic
presentation: Episodic wheeze — variable, worse at night and early morning; characteristic diurnal pattern
Breathlessness and chest tightness — triggered by allergens, cold air, exercise, NSAIDs, beta-blockers
Dry cough — may be the only symptom in cough-variant asthma
Atopic history: eczema, allergic rhinitis, family history of atopy
signs: Expiratory wheeze on auscultation — polyphonic, bilateral
Hyperinflated chest in chronic poorly controlled asthma — barrel chest, increased AP diameter
May be normal between attacks — variable nature of obstruction is key diagnostic feature

bedside: Peak flow: diurnal variation >20% (morning vs evening) on serial measurements over 2 weeks — confirms reversible obstruction
Peak flow: ≥15% improvement after bronchodilator (SABA) — demonstrates reversibility
Spirometry: obstructive pattern (FEV1/FVC <0.70), reversible with bronchodilator (≥12% and ≥200mL improvement in FEV1)
bloods: FBC: eosinophilia (>0.4 × 10⁹/L) — Type 2 inflammatory pattern; guides biological therapy
Total IgE and specific allergen IgE (RAST): elevated in allergic/extrinsic asthma; identifies triggers
FeNO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide): >40 ppb indicates eosinophilic airway inflammation — supports asthma diagnosis and predicts steroid response
imaging: CXR: often normal; hyperinflation in severe/chronic disease; exclude alternative diagnoses (pneumothorax, lobar collapse, foreign body)

mx: Step 1: SABA (short-acting beta-2 agonist e.g. salbutamol) reliever PRN — for symptom relief; using >3x/week indicates need to step up
Step 2: Add inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) — reduces airway inflammation; prevents exacerbations; mainstay of maintenance therapy
Step 3: Add LABA (long-acting beta-2 agonist e.g. salmeterol) to ICS — combined ICS/LABA; never LABA alone in asthma (increased mortality without ICS)
traps: LABA must never be prescribed as monotherapy in asthma (without ICS) — associated with increased asthma deaths; always combined inhaler
NSAIDs and aspirin are contraindicated in aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD/Samter's triad): asthma + nasal polyps + aspirin sensitivity

name: COPD - stable
presentation: Chronic productive cough — persistent, daily, often worse in mornings (bronchitis component)
Progressive exertional dyspnoea — insidious onset over years; often dismissed as 'getting old'
Wheeze — expiratory; less variable than asthma
Smoking history ≥20 pack-years — essential exposure history; also occupational dust/fumes
signs: Barrel chest — increased AP diameter from air trapping; hyperinflation
Pursed lip breathing — auto-PEEP to prevent small airway collapse on expiration
Use of accessory muscles (sternomastoid, scalenes) at rest in severe COPD
Quiet breath sounds bilaterally — hyperinflation reduces transmission
Polyphonic wheeze and prolonged expiratory phase

bedside: Spirometry (post-bronchodilator): FEV1/FVC <0.70 confirms airflow obstruction; FEV1 % predicted grades severity (GOLD 1 ≥80%, GOLD 2 50-79%, GOLD 3 30-49%, GOLD 4 <30%)
SpO2 at rest and on exertion — desaturation on exercise indicates need for LTOT assessment
bloods: FBC: secondary polycythaemia (raised Hb/haematocrit) from chronic hypoxaemia stimulating erythropoietin
Alpha-1 antitrypsin level: if onset <45 years, non-smoker, or family history — deficiency causes panacinar basal emphysema
ABG: type 2 respiratory failure (elevated PaCO2 + low PaO2) in severe disease — confirms CO2 retention before LTOT prescription
imaging: CXR: hyperinflation (>6 anterior ribs in midclavicular line, flat hemidiaphragms, increased retrosternal space), bullae, hyperlucent lung fields — emphysema pattern
HRCT chest: panacinar emphysema (lower lobes — alpha-1-AT deficiency) vs centrilobular (upper lobes — smoking); identifies bullae, excludes lung cancer

mx: Smoking cessation — single most effective intervention; slows FEV1 decline regardless of disease severity
SABA (salbutamol) +/- SAMA (ipratropium) PRN reliever — symptomatic bronchodilation
LAMA (long-acting muscarinic antagonist, e.g. tiotropium) — maintenance bronchodilator; reduces exacerbations and improves quality of life in GOLD 2+
traps: ICS alone is not recommended in COPD (unlike asthma) — only add ICS as third agent with dual bronchodilator (LABA+LAMA) in frequent exacerbators with eosinophils ≥300; risk of pneumonia
Long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT) is only beneficial in COPD if PaO2 ≤7.3 kPa on two ABGs 3 weeks apart when stable — does not improve symptoms but extends survival

name: Community-acquired pneumonia
presentation: Productive cough — purulent sputum, occasionally rust-coloured (Streptococcus pneumoniae)
Fever, rigors, pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worse on inspiration and cough)
Dyspnoea — severity proportional to extent of consolidation
Confusion — especially in elderly; may be the only presenting symptom (atypical presentation)
signs: Dullness to percussion over affected lobe — consolidated airless lung
Increased tactile vocal fremitus and vocal resonance over consolidation (whispered pectoriloquy)
Bronchial breathing — tubular quality, equal inspiratory and expiratory phases, over consolidation
Coarse crackles in affected area
Tachycardia, tachypnoea, fever — systemic inflammatory response

bedside: SpO2: hypoxaemia correlates with severity; SpO2 <92% indicates severe pneumonia requiring hospital admission
Urine dip: Legionella urinary antigen (collected early — positive in first week, remains positive up to 3 months; detects serogroup 1 only)
bloods: CRP: markedly elevated (typically >100 mg/L in bacterial CAP); used in CURB-65 scoring and monitoring treatment response
WBC: neutrophilia in bacterial pneumonia; lymphopenia in viral/atypical
U&E: urea >7 mmol/L is a CURB-65 severity marker; hyponatraemia in Legionella pneumonia
Blood cultures: taken before antibiotics in moderate-severe CAP; positive in ~10-20%
imaging: CXR: lobar or segmental consolidation — homogeneous opacification with air bronchograms (air in bronchi visible against opaque consolidated lung); may lag 24-48h behind clinical presentation
CXR: bilateral patchy consolidation — atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Legionella, viral); parapneumonic effusion (blunted costophrenic angle) indicates complicated pneumonia

mx: Antibiotics guided by severity (CURB-65 score: 0-1 = mild → oral amoxicillin; 2 = moderate → oral amoxicillin + clarithromycin; ≥3 = severe → IV co-amoxiclav + clarithromycin)
Supplemental oxygen — target SpO2 94-98% (88-92% if coexistent COPD)
Physiotherapy and adequate hydration — promotes sputum clearance; fluid resuscitation if septic
traps: Atypical organisms (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella) do not respond to beta-lactam antibiotics alone — always add a macrolide (clarithromycin) for moderate-severe CAP or atypical features

Failure to improve after 48-72h on antibiotics — consider parapneumonic effusion/empyema (needs USS ± pleural aspiration), resistant organism, alternative diagnosis (PE, lung cancer with post-obstructive pneumonia)

name: Pulmonary embolism
presentation: Acute dyspnoea — sudden onset at rest, often without clear precipitant
Pleuritic chest pain — suggests peripheral PE with pulmonary infarction (pleural irritation)
Haemoptysis — usually small volume, suggests pulmonary infarction
Risk factors: recent surgery/immobility, long-haul flight, malignancy, OCP, pregnancy, prior VTE, thrombophilia
signs: Tachycardia and tachypnoea — most sensitive signs; tachycardia >100 bpm in majority
Hypoxia — SpO2 reduced; may maintain SpO2 at rest but desaturate on exertion
Signs of DVT: unilateral calf tenderness, swelling, erythema — present in ~30%
Massive PE: hypotension, syncope, raised JVP (right heart strain), loud P2 — haemodynamic compromise

bedside: ECG: sinus tachycardia (commonest); S1Q3T3 pattern (S wave lead I, Q wave and T inversion lead III) — specific but insensitive; right heart strain: RBBB, right axis deviation, T-wave inversion V1-V4
SpO2: hypoxia at rest or desaturation on exertion
bloods: D-dimer: elevated (>500 ng/mL) — high sensitivity, low specificity; use only if pre-test probability LOW (Wells ≤4); negative D-dimer effectively excludes PE in low probability; NOT useful if high probability
ABG: type 1 respiratory failure (low PaO2, low PaCO2 from hyperventilation), raised A-a gradient
Troponin and BNP: elevated in massive/submassive PE from right heart strain — prognostic markers
imaging: CTPA (CT pulmonary angiogram): gold standard — filling defect within pulmonary artery; shows clot burden, right heart strain (RV:LV ratio >0.9); also identifies alternative diagnoses

CXR: often normal (most important role is to exclude alternative diagnoses); classic but rare signs: Hampton's hump (wedge-shaped peripheral opacity from infarction), Westermark sign (oligaemia distal to embolus)

mx: Anticoagulation — immediate LMWH or DOAC (apixaban or rivaroxaban — weight-based initial dose); prevents clot propagation and allows endogenous fibrinolysis
Duration of anticoagulation — provoked PE (transient risk factor): 3 months; unprovoked PE: ≥3-6 months, consider indefinite; active cancer: LMWH or DOAC for duration of cancer treatment
Massive PE with haemodynamic instability — systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) or surgical/catheter embolectomy; risks of thrombolysis (major bleeding) must be weighed against mortality risk
traps: D-dimer is only useful when pre-test probability is LOW — a positive D-dimer in a high-probability patient adds nothing and should not delay CTPA; and D-dimer is elevated in many conditions (sepsis, malignancy, pregnancy, post-surgery)

Massive PE with hypotension: do NOT give aggressive IV fluids — right heart is already dilated and volume loading worsens RV failure and shifts interventricular septum (D-sign on echo) impairing LV filling

name: C. difficile
presentation: Profuse watery diarrhoea (≥3 loose stools/24h), often offensive-smelling
Lower abdominal cramping pain
Fever and systemic upset
Recent antibiotic exposure (classically clindamycin, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones, co-amoxiclav)
Healthcare or care-home exposure within preceding 12 weeks
signs: Diffuse lower abdominal tenderness on palpation
Abdominal distension (raises concern for toxic megacolon)
Fever and tachycardia indicating systemic sepsis
Signs of dehydration: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, postural hypotension

bedside: Stool sample: glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) antigen screen — sensitive for C. difficile presence; if positive proceed to toxin
Stool toxin EIA (toxin A/B): confirms toxin-producing strain; high specificity, lower sensitivity — negative toxin with positive GDH = non-toxigenic or send for NAAT
Stool NAAT/PCR: most sensitive for toxin genes; use if GDH positive but toxin negative
Observations: HR, BP, temperature, respiratory rate — severity scoring (WCC >15, creatinine rise, fever >38.5°C = severe)
bloods: FBC: leucocytosis (WCC >15 × 10⁹/L indicates severe disease)
U&Es: acute kidney injury from dehydration; rising creatinine is a severity marker
CRP: elevated, correlates with inflammation; very high (>150) suggests severe/complicated disease
Serum lactate: elevated in toxic megacolon or septic shock — triggers urgent surgical review
imaging: AXR: colonic dilatation >6 cm (transverse colon) indicates toxic megacolon; mucosal oedema ('thumbprinting')
CT abdomen/pelvis (if severe or deteriorating): pancolitis, pericolic fat stranding, colonic wall thickening, perforation
AXR or CT showing colonic diameter >9 cm (caecum) = surgical emergency

mx: Stop implicated antibiotic if clinically safe to do so — reduces ongoing C. difficile proliferation
Oral vancomycin (glycopeptide) — now first-line over metronidazole for all episodes; poorly absorbed so achieves high intraluminal concentration targeting C. difficile directly
Fidaxomicin (macrocyclic antibiotic) — preferred over vancomycin for first recurrence or high recurrence risk; narrower spectrum, lower recurrence rate
IV metronidazole (nitroimidazole) added if ileus or toxic megacolon prevents enteral drug delivery; oral metronidazole no longer first-line for initial episode
Faecal microbiota transplant (FMT) — for multiply recurrent C. difficile; restores colonisation resistance
Surgical colectomy (subtotal) reserved for toxic megacolon with perforation or failure to respond to maximal medical therapy
traps: Metronidazole is no longer first-line — vancomycin oral is now recommended first-line for initial and severe episodes per UK guidelines; using metronidazole first is a common exam error
A positive GDH antigen alone does not confirm toxigenic infection — toxin EIA or NAAT is required; GDH is a screening test only
Antidiarrhoeal agents (loperamide) are contraindicated — they reduce gut motility, increasing toxin retention and risk of toxic megacolon
Proton pump inhibitors are an independent risk factor for C. difficile acquisition; review and discontinue if not essential
Recurrence (within 8 weeks) is common (~20%) and is not treatment failure — it requires repeat testing and escalation to fidaxomicin or FMT, not simply re-treating with the same agent

name: Crohn's disease
presentation: Crampy right iliac fossa pain (terminal ileum most common site)
Diarrhoea — often without blood (unlike UC), may be steatorrhoeic if ileal disease
Weight loss and malnutrition (malabsorption + anorexia)
Perianal disease: fistulae, skin tags, abscesses
Extraintestinal: erythema nodosum, uveitis, primary sclerosing cholangitis (less common than UC), seronegative arthropathy
signs: Right iliac fossa mass or tenderness (inflamed/matted terminal ileum)
Perianal fistulae, skin tags, or anal fissures on inspection
Mouth: aphthous ulcers (cobblestoning of oral mucosa)
Cachexia and pallor — reflecting chronic malabsorption and anaemia

bedside: Stool MC&S — exclude infective cause (Clostridioides difficile, Campylobacter) before diagnosing flare
Faecal calprotectin — elevated (>250 µg/g); useful to distinguish IBD from IBS and to monitor activity
bloods: FBC: normocytic or macrocytic anaemia (B12/folate malabsorption from ileal disease); thrombocytosis in active disease
CRP and ESR: elevated — markers of systemic inflammation and disease activity
LFTs + albumin: hypoalbuminaemia reflects malnutrition/protein-losing enteropathy
B12 and folate: low B12 specific to terminal ileal Crohn's; folate low if proximal small bowel involved or methotrexate used
imaging: Colonoscopy + biopsy: skip lesions (discontinuous inflammation) with deep rose-thorn ulcers, cobblestoning of mucosa, and rectal sparing; biopsy shows transmural inflammation and non-caseating granulomas
MRI enterography (MRE): gold standard for small bowel assessment — shows mural thickening, mucosal hyperenhancement, mesenteric fat stranding ('creeping fat'), and fistula tracts
CT abdomen/pelvis: used acutely — identifies complications including abscess, obstruction, and free perforation; skip lesions and bowel wall thickening visible

mx: Induce remission: corticosteroids (prednisolone or budesonide) — anti-inflammatory; budesonide preferred for ileocaecal disease due to lower systemic side-effect profile
Maintain remission: thiopurines (azathioprine/6-mercaptopurine) as steroid-sparing agents; methotrexate if thiopurine-intolerant

Biologics: anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) for moderate-severe or fistulating disease not responding to immunomodulators; vedolizumab (anti-integrin) or ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23) as alternatives
traps: Rectal sparing in Crohn's vs. rectal involvement always in UC — examiners test this; if rectum involved and continuous, think UC
Crohn's can cause bloody diarrhoea (colonic disease) — bloody stool does NOT rule out Crohn's or confirm UC
Terminal ileal disease causes B12 malabsorption (not folate); UC does not affect B12 — low B12 points to Crohn's
Granulomas on biopsy are present in only ~60% of Crohn's — their absence does not exclude the diagnosis; transmural inflammation and skip lesions are the key histological/endoscopic hallmarks

name: GORD
presentation: Retrosternal burning (heartburn) worse after meals, bending, lying flat
Acid regurgitation — sour/bitter taste reaching throat or mouth
Dysphagia (alarm feature if present — suggests stricture or Barrett's)
Nocturnal cough or wheeze — acid micro-aspiration
Globus sensation — feeling of lump in throat
signs: Often no signs on examination
Epigastric tenderness on deep palpation
Dental erosion of enamel (chronic acid exposure)
Hoarseness (laryngopharyngeal reflux)

bedside: Urine dip — to exclude UTI/renal cause of atypical chest/back pain
ECG — exclude cardiac ischaemia in chest-pain presentations
Symptom diary / response to empirical PPI trial (diagnostic in typical uncomplicated cases)
bloods: FBC — haemoglobin normal (anaemia would suggest bleeding/Barrett's complication)
H. pylori serology or stool antigen — positive in co-existent H. pylori infection
LFTs and U&Es — normal, used to exclude hepatobiliary or metabolic causes
imaging: Upper GI endoscopy: erythema, erosions or ulceration at gastro-oesophageal junction; Los Angeles classification grades A–D of oesophagitis; may show columnar-lined epithelium (salmon-pink tongues) indicating Barrett's oesophagus
24-hour ambulatory pH-impedance monitoring: acid exposure time >4% of study period; correlates symptom episodes with pH drops <4 — gold standard for diagnosis
Oesophageal manometry: normal or reduced lower oesophageal sphincter (LOS) resting pressure (<10 mmHg); used pre-operatively to exclude dysmotility before fundoplication

mx: Lifestyle: head-of-bed elevation, weight loss, avoid trigger foods (fat, caffeine, alcohol, chocolate), avoid eating within 3 hours of lying down
Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs, e.g. omeprazole) — irreversibly block H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, most effective acid suppression; first-line for oesophagitis
H2-receptor antagonists (H2RAs, e.g. ranitidine/famotidine) — competitive histamine blockade reducing acid secretion; second-line or add-on for nocturnal breakthrough
Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication — surgical reinforcement of LOS for refractory disease or patients wishing to stop long-term medication
traps: Atypical chest pain mimicking ACS — always exclude cardiac cause before attributing to GORD; ECG and troponin first
Dysphagia is a red-flag symptom requiring urgent endoscopy regardless of GORD history — do not attribute to benign reflux without investigation
Chronic GORD can progress to Barrett's oesophagus (metaplastic columnar epithelium) → oesophageal adenocarcinoma; surveillance endoscopy indicated
PPIs can mask symptoms of underlying gastric or oesophageal malignancy — ensure endoscopy before starting long-term PPI in patients over 55 or with alarm features

name: H. pylori
presentation: Epigastric pain or burning, often relieved by eating (duodenal ulcer pattern) or worsened by eating (gastric ulcer pattern)
Nausea, with or without vomiting
Bloating and early satiety
Haematemesis or melaena if complicated by peptic ulcer bleeding
Often asymptomatic — incidental finding on investigation for dyspepsia
signs: Epigastric tenderness on deep palpation
Signs of anaemia (pallor, tachycardia) if chronic GI blood loss
Rarely: peritonitic abdomen if perforation (guarding, rigidity, rebound)

bedside: Urea breath test (13C-UBT): positive — labelled CO2 detected in exhaled breath after ingestion of 13C-labelled urea, indicating urease-producing H. pylori
Stool antigen test (SAT): positive — H. pylori antigen detected in faeces; used for diagnosis and to confirm eradication
Faecal occult blood: positive if active ulcer bleeding
bloods: FBC: microcytic hypochromic anaemia (low Hb, low MCV, low MCH) if chronic blood loss from peptic ulceration
H. pylori serology (IgG): positive — detects prior exposure but cannot distinguish active from cleared infection; not recommended for eradication testing
U&E and clotting: typically normal unless severe haemorrhage or complication
LFTs: normal — abnormal suggests alternative diagnosis
imaging: OGD (endoscopy): visualises gastric or duodenal ulcer — clean-based or with stigmata of haemorrhage (visible vessel, adherent clot); antral erythema and nodularity typical of H. pylori gastritis; biopsy (CLO test / histology) confirms organism
Barium meal (historical): crater-like ulcer niche projecting from duodenal cap or lesser curve of stomach — now largely replaced by OGD
CXR (if perforation suspected): free air under diaphragm on erect film

mx: Triple therapy (7–14 days): PPI (proton pump inhibitor — suppresses acid to allow mucosal healing and improve antibiotic efficacy) + clarithromycin (macrolide — bactericidal) + amoxicillin (penicillin — bactericidal); metronidazole substituted if penicillin allergy
Bismuth-based quadruple therapy: reserved for clarithromycin-resistant or treatment-failure cases — PPI + bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole
Confirm eradication: repeat urea breath test or stool antigen test at least 4 weeks after completing antibiotics and 2 weeks after stopping PPI to avoid false-negative
traps: Serology cannot confirm eradication — IgG remains positive for months after successful treatment; always use UBT or SAT for test-of-cure
PPIs must be stopped 2 weeks before UBT or SAT — they suppress urease activity and cause false-negative results
NSAIDs and H. pylori are independent risk factors for peptic ulcer disease — eradicating H. pylori does not remove NSAID risk; both must be addressed
Gastric ulcers require repeat endoscopy at 6–8 weeks to confirm healing and exclude malignancy — duodenal ulcers do not routinely need repeat OGD

name: Hepatic encephalopathy
presentation: Grade I: subtle personality change, reversed sleep-wake cycle, mild confusion
Grade II: disorientation, inappropriate behaviour, asterixis present
Grade III: gross disorientation, stupor, barely rousable but responds to stimuli
Grade IV: coma — unresponsive to verbal or painful stimuli
Precipitant often identifiable: GI bleed, infection, constipation, sedatives, electrolyte disturbance
signs: Asterixis (flapping tremor) — metabolic encephalopathy hallmark; absent in deep coma
Fetor hepaticus — sweet musty breath from mercaptans; indicates severe hepatocellular failure
Jaundice and stigmata of chronic liver disease (spider naevi, palmar erythema, leuconychia)
Reduced conscious level graded by GCS; may show frontal release signs in advanced disease

bedside: Number connection test (Trail Making Test A) — sensitive bedside screen for covert/minimal HE
EEG — diffuse slowing and triphasic waves in overt HE; not routine but useful in atypical cases
Observations + blood glucose — hypoglycaemia mimics HE and must be excluded immediately
bloods: Serum ammonia — elevated in most cases, but level correlates poorly with grade; a normal ammonia does not exclude HE and a raised ammonia alone does not confirm it
LFTs + bilirubin — deranged, reflect underlying hepatic dysfunction
FBC, CRP, cultures — identify infective precipitant (SBP, UTI, pneumonia)
U&E + clotting (PT/INR) — electrolyte disturbance (hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia) precipitates HE; coagulopathy reflects synthetic failure
imaging: USS abdomen with Doppler — portal hypertension, splenomegaly, ascites, hepatic vasculature patency
CT brain (non-contrast) — exclude structural cause (subdural haematoma, stroke) before attributing altered consciousness to HE
CT/MRI brain in chronic HE — bilateral basal ganglia T1 hyperintensity from manganese deposition (incidental finding)

mx: Identify and treat precipitant first — GI bleed (PPI + terlipressin), infection (broad-spectrum antibiotics), constipation, electrolyte correction
Lactulose (non-absorbable disaccharide) — reduces colonic pH, traps ammonia as NH4+, promotes bowel transit; titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day
Rifaximin (minimally absorbed oral antibiotic) — reduces ammonia-producing gut flora; used as secondary prophylaxis in recurrent HE alongside lactulose
traps: Ammonia level caveat — serum ammonia is not a reliable severity marker; a raised level supports the diagnosis but does not grade HE, and treatment targets are clinical (West Haven grade), not biochemical

Protein restriction is outdated — older teaching advocated low-protein diet; current evidence shows adequate protein intake (1.2-1.5 g/kg/day) is essential and restriction worsens sarcopenia and outcomes

Always exclude structural brain pathology — subdural haematoma is common in cirrhotic patients (coagulopathy + falls); never diagnose HE without excluding an intracranial cause when there is diagnostic uncertainty

Sedatives as precipitant — benzodiazepines and opioids are dramatically potentiated in liver failure; look for a drug history in any decompensation and avoid these agents; flumazenil may transiently improve HE (endogenous benzodiazepine hypothesis)

name: Peptic ulcer bleed
presentation: Haematemesis (bright red or coffee-ground vomiting)
Melaena (black, tarry, offensive stools)
Epigastric pain preceding bleed (classically relieved by food in duodenal ulcer)
Syncope or presyncope from acute blood loss
Haematochezia if massive bleed (rapid transit)
signs: Tachycardia and hypotension (haemodynamic compromise — severity marker)
Pallor and cold peripheries (acute anaemia / poor perfusion)
Epigastric tenderness on palpation
Melaena on digital rectal examination

bedside: Obs + postural BP drop (>20 mmHg systolic drop suggests significant volume loss)
Digital rectal examination — confirm melaena, exclude rectal source
Glasgow-Blatchford Score at bedside (pre-endoscopy risk stratification; score ≥1 = inpatient endoscopy)
bloods: FBC — low Hb (anaemia; may be normal early before haemodilution), raised WCC if perforation
U&E — raised urea disproportionate to creatinine (blood digested in gut → urea absorbed; urea:creatinine ratio >100:1)
Clotting (PT/APTT) — prolonged if coagulopathy or anticoagulant use
Group and save / crossmatch — prepare for transfusion
imaging: OGD (upper GI endoscopy) within 24 h (or <12 h if haemodynamically unstable after resuscitation) — diagnostic and therapeutic

Forrest classification on OGD: Ia spurting arterial bleed, Ib oozing, IIa visible vessel, IIb adherent clot, IIc flat pigmented spot, III clean-based ulcer (I–IIb = high rebleed risk; endoscopic treatment indicated)
CT angiography if endoscopy non-diagnostic or patient too unstable for OGD — localise bleeding point for interventional radiology

mx: Resuscitation first: IV access ×2 wide-bore, crystalloid then blood transfusion (target Hb >70 g/L, >80 g/L if cardiovascular disease); correct coagulopathy with FFP/platelets
IV proton pump inhibitor (PPI) — high-dose PPI infusion post-endoscopy to raise intragastric pH, stabilise clot, reduce rebleed risk (class: proton pump inhibitor)
Endoscopic haemostasis at OGD — injection (adrenaline), thermal coagulation, or mechanical clip for Forrest Ia/Ib/IIa/IIb lesions
H. pylori test-and-treat (urea breath test or biopsy CLO test at endoscopy) — eradication reduces recurrence; use triple therapy (PPI + two antibiotics)
traps: Normal Hb on admission does not exclude significant bleed — haemodilution takes hours; serial FBC required
Raised urea with normal creatinine is a hallmark of upper GI bleed (blood as protein load) — do not attribute to renal impairment alone
NSAIDs and aspirin are the commonest precipitants — always ask about analgesic/antiplatelet use and stop/review on admission
Forrest III (clean-based ulcer) is low rebleed risk and does not require endoscopic treatment — overtreating risks perforation

name: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis
presentation: Fever and rigors in a patient with known ascites
Diffuse abdominal pain or tenderness
Acute confusion or encephalopathy (may be only feature in 10%)
Deteriorating renal function without clear cause
Worsening ascites despite diuretics
signs: Diffuse abdominal tenderness on palpation — guarding may be absent in tense ascites
Shifting dullness / fluid thrill confirming ascites
Fever (>38°C) — may be low-grade or absent in immunosuppressed
Signs of decompensated liver disease: jaundice, encephalopathy, spider naevi

bedside: Diagnostic ascitic tap — PMN (neutrophil) count ≥250 cells/mm³ confirms SBP regardless of culture result
Ascitic fluid culture — inoculate blood culture bottles at bedside to improve yield; most commonly E. coli, Klebsiella, Streptococcus pneumoniae
Ascitic fluid appearance — turbid/cloudy fluid raises suspicion; bloody tap should trigger haemorrhagic ascites differential
bloods: FBC — raised WCC (neutrophilia) supports infection; thrombocytopenia common in cirrhosis
CRP and blood cultures — CRP elevated; blood cultures positive in ~50% of SBP
U&E and creatinine — renal impairment predicts poor prognosis; monitors for hepatorenal syndrome
LFTs and clotting (INR) — baseline liver function; coagulopathy affects procedural risk
imaging: USS abdomen — confirms ascites, guides ascitic tap if loculated; excludes secondary peritonitis (free gas, abscess, perforation)
CT abdomen/pelvis — if secondary peritonitis suspected (i.e. ascitic PMN >250 but polymicrobial culture, very high protein or LDH) to identify surgical source

mx: Empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics — third-generation cephalosporin (e.g. cefotaxime class) first-line; covers gram-negative enteric organisms
IV human albumin — given at diagnosis and day 3 to reduce risk of hepatorenal syndrome (HRS); class: plasma volume expander/oncotic agent
Secondary prophylaxis with oral fluoroquinolone (e.g. norfloxacin class) or co-trimoxazole — lifelong after first episode to prevent recurrence
traps: Culture-negative SBP — PMN ≥250 cells/mm³ IS sufficient to diagnose and treat SBP even if cultures return negative; do not withhold antibiotics

Secondary peritonitis masquerading as SBP — suspect if cultures are polymicrobial, ascitic protein >10 g/L, glucose <2.8 mmol/L, or LDH >upper limit of normal; requires surgical/CT workup not antibiotics alone
Primary prophylaxis indications — offer to patients with ascitic protein <15 g/L plus either Child-Pugh ≥9 or impaired renal function; commonly missed in exams
Hepatorenal syndrome risk — failing to give albumin alongside antibiotics is a classic omission; albumin reduces HRS and mortality, not just restoring fluid

name: UC - maintaining remission
presentation: Asymptomatic between relapses — formed stool, no blood, normal frequency
Relapse heralded by return of bloody diarrhoea, urgency, and nocturnal stooling
Fatigue and weight loss may signal subclinical inflammation or iron deficiency
Extra-intestinal manifestations (uveitis, erythema nodosum, primary sclerosing cholangitis) can flare independently of bowel disease
signs: Examination typically normal in established remission
Pallor suggesting anaemia of chronic disease or iron deficiency from prior bleeds
Aphthous mouth ulcers — associated extra-intestinal feature
Jaundice or hepatomegaly if coexisting primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC)

bedside: Stool calprotectin — elevated (>250 µg/g) indicates mucosal inflammation and impending relapse before symptoms develop
Stool culture — exclude infective trigger (Clostridioides difficile, Campylobacter) if apparent relapse
Abdominal examination and PR if active bleeding suspected
bloods: FBC — monitor for thiopurine-induced myelosuppression (leucopenia, thrombocytopenia); anaemia from chronic disease
LFTs — thiopurines can cause hepatotoxicity; check for PSC-related cholestasis
Thiopurine methyltransferase (TPMT) activity — must be checked before starting azathioprine or mercaptopurine; low/absent TPMT → risk of severe myelosuppression
Thioguanine nucleotide (TGN) metabolite levels — confirms adherence and therapeutic range if inadequate response
CRP and ESR — trend markers of systemic inflammation; raised in relapse
imaging: Colonoscopy with biopsies — surveillance for dysplasia and colorectal cancer; frequency determined by extent, duration, and PSC co-existence
Chromoendoscopy or dye-spray colonoscopy — preferred over random biopsies for dysplasia detection in long-standing disease
MRI abdomen/pelvis — assess for PSC or small-bowel Crohn if diagnosis in doubt; not routine for UC surveillance

mx: Aminosalicylates (5-ASA, e.g. mesalazine) — first-line maintenance for mild-to-moderate UC; reduces mucosal inflammation via topical anti-inflammatory action; oral ± rectal route depending on disease extent
Thiopurines (azathioprine or mercaptopurine) — for steroid-dependent or steroid-refractory UC; immunomodulator; must check TPMT before starting and monitor FBC/LFTs regularly

Biologics (anti-TNF e.g. infliximab, vedolizumab, ustekinumab) — for refractory or biologic-dependent disease; vedolizumab is gut-selective (anti-integrin) and preferred in UC; combination with thiopurine reduces immunogenicity of anti-TNF
traps: TPMT testing is mandatory before starting any thiopurine — low or absent TPMT activity causes accumulation of active metabolites → life-threatening myelosuppression; intermediate activity requires dose reduction

Colorectal cancer surveillance intervals: extensive colitis (beyond splenic flexure) → colonoscopy at 8 years from diagnosis, then every 1–5 years based on risk stratification (inflammation grade, PSC, family history); PSC + UC → annual colonoscopy from diagnosis
5-ASA alone is insufficient maintenance for patients who required steroids to achieve remission — failing to step up to thiopurine or biologic is a common exam omission
Methotrexate is NOT effective for maintaining remission in UC (unlike Crohn's disease) — a classic cross-condition trap in finals

name: Ulcerative colitis
presentation: Bloody diarrhoea with mucus — hallmark; frequency reflects severity
Urgency and tenesmus — rectal inflammation drives constant sensation of need to defecate
Cramping lower abdominal pain — typically left-sided, relieved by defecation
Fatigue and weight loss — chronic inflammation and nutritional deficiency
Extraintestinal features — uveitis, erythema nodosum, primary sclerosing cholangitis
signs: Lower abdominal tenderness on palpation — particularly left iliac fossa in left-sided disease
Pyrexia and tachycardia — indicate severe/fulminant flare; trigger Truelove & Witts criteria
Perianal inspection: no fistulae or skin tags (distinguishes from Crohn's)
Pallor — iron-deficiency anaemia secondary to chronic blood loss

bedside: Stool chart: record frequency and Bristol type; blood/mucus presence; send stool MC&S to exclude infective cause (Clostridioides difficile, Salmonella, Campylobacter)
Observations: HR >90, temp >37.8 °C, or >6 bloody stools/day = severe flare by Truelove & Witts criteria

AXR: colonic dilatation >6 cm in transverse colon indicates toxic megacolon; mucosal islands (pseudopolyps) on plain film in severe disease; loss of haustral pattern (lead-pipe colon) in long-standing disease
bloods: FBC: normocytic or microcytic anaemia; raised WCC in severe flare; thrombocytosis (reactive)
CRP and ESR: elevated proportional to disease activity; CRP >45 at 3 days predicts need for colectomy
U&E: hypokalaemia and hypoalbuminaemia in severe disease from diarrhoeal losses and inflammation
LFTs including GGT: screen for primary sclerosing cholangitis (raised ALP/GGT disproportionate to other LFTs)
imaging: AXR (first-line in acute severe UC): assess for toxic megacolon (transverse colon >6 cm); mucosal thickening; absence of faecal loading in affected colon

Colonoscopy + biopsy (gold standard, not in acute severe flare — perforation risk): continuous mucosal inflammation starting at rectum extending proximally; erythema, granularity, friability, contact bleeding, and superficial ulceration; crypt abscesses and goblet cell depletion on histology
CT abdomen/pelvis (if colonoscopy unsafe in severe flare): mural thickening of colon, pericolonic fat stranding, assess for perforation or abscess

mx: Mild-moderate flare: topical aminosalicylates (5-ASA, e.g. mesalazine suppository/enema) — reduce mucosal inflammation via prostaglandin inhibition; oral 5-ASA added for left-sided or extensive disease
Moderate-severe flare: oral corticosteroids (prednisolone) — broad immunosuppression to induce remission; taper once in remission; not used as maintenance

Acute severe UC (Truelove & Witts severe criteria): admit, IV corticosteroids (hydrocortisone) — if no response by 72 hours, consider IV ciclosporin (calcineurin inhibitor) or anti-TNF (infliximab) as rescue therapy, or refer for colectomy
traps: Sending stool cultures is mandatory before starting steroids — infective colitis (especially C. difficile) mimics a flare and steroids will worsen infection
Do not perform colonoscopy in acute severe UC — risk of perforation; use AXR and CT instead to assess extent and complications
UC does not cause skip lesions, fistulae, or perianal disease — these features point to Crohn's; examiners test this distinction
PSC association is with UC not Crohn's — a candidate who attributes raised ALP to Crohn's disease will lose marks; always check LFTs in UC and screen for PSC

name: Addisonian crisis
presentation: Profound weakness and fatigue, often preceding collapse
Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain (may mimic acute abdomen)
Postural dizziness or frank syncope
Altered consciousness or confusion in severe cases
Precipitant identifiable: intercurrent illness, surgery, steroid withdrawal, trauma
signs: Postural or supine hypotension (circulatory shock in severe cases)
Hyperpigmentation of skin creases, buccal mucosa, and scars (primary Addison's only)
Signs of dehydration: reduced skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, tachycardia
Pyrexia if sepsis is the precipitant

bedside: Obs: hypotension, tachycardia, temperature (identify septic precipitant)
BM: hypoglycaemia — a cardinal feature, may be profound
ECG: peaked T-waves or broad QRS if severe hyperkalaemia
bloods: U&E: hyponatraemia (↓Na) and hyperkalaemia (↑K) — the classic electrolyte pattern
Glucose: hypoglycaemia (↓glucose) — completes the triad
Serum cortisol: take before hydrocortisone if possible, but do not delay treatment
Short Synacthen test (SST): 250 mcg IM/IV tetracosactide — cortisol fails to rise adequately (peak <550 nmol/L); performed after stabilisation, not acutely
imaging: CT abdomen/adrenals: if bilateral adrenal haemorrhage or Waterhouse–Friderichsen syndrome suspected
CXR: to identify precipitating illness (pneumonia, TB)

mx: IV hydrocortisone (glucocorticoid replacement) — treat on clinical suspicion before results; take cortisol sample first if it causes no delay
IV 0.9% saline with glucose: correct hypovolaemia and hypoglycaemia simultaneously
Identify and treat precipitant (e.g. antibiotics for sepsis); do not withhold steroids pending diagnosis
traps: Treat on suspicion — do not wait for cortisol results; delay is life-threatening
Synacthen test is done after stabilisation, never during the acute crisis
Fludrocortisone (mineralocorticoid) is not needed acutely — high-dose hydrocortisone has sufficient mineralocorticoid activity
Sick day rules on discharge: patients must double or triple oral steroid dose during illness and carry an emergency hydrocortisone injection

name: DKA
presentation: Vomiting, nausea, abdominal pain
Polyuria and polydipsia (osmotic symptoms)
Kussmaul breathing (deep sighing respirations — respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis)
Precipitant history: missed insulin, infection (UTI/LRTI), new T1DM presentation, alcohol
signs: Kussmaul respirations — deep, sighing, rapid breathing
Ketotic (pear-drop/acetone) breath
Dehydration — dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, tachycardia, hypotension
Reduced GCS or confusion in severe cases

bedside: BM (capillary glucose) — typically >11 mmol/L (may be lower in euglycaemic DKA)
Urine dip — glycosuria ++ and ketonuria ++
ABG — pH <7.3, bicarbonate <15 mmol/L confirms acidosis; pCO2 low (respiratory compensation)
bloods: Serum glucose >11 mmol/L (or known diabetes)
Serum/blood ketones >3 mmol/L (diagnostic threshold)
Venous pH <7.3 and bicarbonate <15 mmol/L (severe: pH <7.1, bicarb <5)
U&Es — potassium may be high initially (acidosis shifts K+ extracellularly) but total body K+ depleted; monitor closely
imaging: CXR — identify infective precipitant (consolidation/pneumonia)
ECG — assess hyperkalaemia changes (peaked T waves, wide QRS) or arrhythmia

mx: IV fluid resuscitation first — 0.9% sodium chloride bolus to restore circulating volume before insulin
Fixed-rate IV insulin infusion (FRIII) — started after fluids; drives glucose and ketones down
Potassium replacement — add K+ to fluids once serum K+ <5.5 mmol/L; never give insulin without K+ available as insulin drives K+ into cells causing dangerous hypokalaemia
traps: Euglycaemic DKA — glucose may be normal (<11 mmol/L) especially in SGLT2 inhibitor use or pregnancy; do not rule out DKA on glucose alone
K+ paradox — initial hyperkalaemia masked by total body K+ depletion; once insulin starts, K+ drops rapidly and must be replaced
Start fluids before insulin — giving insulin first without volume resuscitation risks cardiovascular collapse
Cerebral oedema risk — commonest in children; avoid over-rapid fluid correction

name: Hyperkalaemia
presentation: Often asymptomatic — discovered incidentally on bloods
Muscle weakness and fatigue (generalised, ascending)
Palpitations or cardiac arrest (severe — K+ >6.5 mmol/L)
Paraesthesia or flaccid paralysis (very severe)
Nausea, vomiting (non-specific)
signs: Generalised flaccid weakness or hypotonia
Absent or reduced deep tendon reflexes
Bradycardia or irregular pulse (cardiac toxicity)
Signs of underlying cause — Cushingoid features (exogenous steroids), uraemic fetor/pallor (renal failure), hyperpigmentation/postural hypotension (Addison's)

bedside: ECG — mandatory first step; progression with rising K+: peaked (tall, narrow, symmetrical) T waves (K+ >5.5 mmol/L) → prolonged PR interval → flattened/absent P waves (K+ >6.5 mmol/L) → widened QRS / sinusoidal sine wave pattern (K+ >7.0 mmol/L) → VF / asystole (K+ >8.0 mmol/L)
Urine dip — proteinuria/haematuria suggests renal cause; dipstick for myoglobin if rhabdomyolysis
Blood glucose (bedside) — exclude DKA-associated hyperkalaemia (insulin deficiency)
bloods: Serum potassium — confirm level (repeat if haemolysed sample suspected); >5.5 mmol/L = hyperkalaemia, >6.5 mmol/L = severe
Urea and creatinine — AKI or CKD as commonest cause; eGFR to stage renal impairment
Venous blood gas — assess metabolic acidosis (acidosis drives K+ extracellularly); bicarbonate level
Aldosterone and renin (if no obvious cause) — Addison's disease (low aldosterone, low cortisol, high ACTH) or hypoaldosteronism
Cortisol / short Synacthen test — if Addison's suspected (postural hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia)
FBC — haemolysis (high K+ with high LDH, low haptoglobin) or rhabdomyolysis (high CK)
imaging: Renal USS — if AKI suspected; exclude obstructive uropathy (bilateral hydronephrosis); assess renal size/cortex for CKD
CT KUB (non-contrast) — if renal tract obstruction suspected and USS inconclusive

mx: Membrane stabilisation (immediate, cardiac protection): IV calcium gluconate 10% — stabilises cardiac membrane within minutes; does not lower K+; repeat ECG to confirm effect; use first if ECG changes present
Shift K+ into cells (rapid, 15-30 min): IV insulin (actrapid) + 50% dextrose — drives K+ intracellularly; nebulised salbutamol (beta-2 agonist) adjunct; sodium bicarbonate if severe acidosis

Remove K+ from body (definitive): furosemide (loop diuretic, if euvolaemic/volume-overloaded and adequate renal function); calcium resonium (cation-exchange resin, oral/rectal); renal replacement therapy (haemodialysis) for refractory or oliguric AKI/CKD
Stop precipitants: review and stop ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, amiloride), NSAIDs, trimethoprim, heparin
traps: Calcium gluconate protects the heart but does NOT lower serum potassium — it is membrane stabilisation only; you must still shift and remove K+ separately

Pseudohyperkalaemia — haemolysed sample (tight tourniquet, delayed processing, small vein) gives falsely high K+; always repeat if no ECG changes and asymptomatic; true hyperkalaemia has corresponding ECG findings
ECG can be normal even with K+ 6.0-6.5 mmol/L — absence of ECG changes does not exclude dangerous hyperkalaemia; treat on the blood result

Addison's disease — classic cause of hyperkalaemia + hyponatraemia; hyperpigmentation and postural hypotension are the exam clues; do not give insulin/dextrose before cortisol replacement in adrenal crisis as this can precipitate cardiovascular collapse

name: Hyperthyroidism / Graves
presentation: Heat intolerance and sweating
Unintentional weight loss despite increased appetite
Palpitations (AF or sinus tachycardia)
Diarrhoea / increased bowel frequency
Anxiety, irritability, and tremor
signs: Exophthalmos and lid lag (Graves-specific — due to retro-orbital inflammation)
Pretibial myxoedema (Graves-specific — indurated, violaceous plaques on shins)
Diffuse smooth goitre with thyroid bruit (Graves) vs. irregular nodular goitre (toxic MNG)
Fine resting tremor and warm, moist peripheries

bedside: Obs: resting tachycardia or irregularly irregular pulse (AF)
ECG: sinus tachycardia; AF in ~10-15% — check rate and rhythm
bloods: TFTs: TSH suppressed (<0.1 mU/L) + elevated free T4 and/or free T3 (T3 toxicosis if T4 normal)
TSH-receptor antibodies (TRAb / TSI): positive in Graves disease (>95% sensitivity)
FBC before starting carbimazole — baseline for agranulocytosis monitoring
LFTs: carbimazole can cause hepatotoxicity; also mildly deranged in thyrotoxicosis
imaging: USS thyroid: diffuse hypoechoic gland with increased vascularity (Graves) vs. discrete nodule(s) (toxic nodular)

Isotope scan (Tc-99m or I-123): diffuse homogeneous uptake = Graves; focal hot nodule(s) with suppressed surrounding tissue = toxic adenoma / toxic MNG — key distinction when diagnosis unclear or pre-radioiodine

mx: Symptom control: non-selective beta-blocker (propranolol) — controls tachycardia, tremor, and anxiety while awaiting biochemical response
Antithyroid drug: carbimazole (titration or block-and-replace) — first-line in UK; propylthiouracil (PTU) preferred in first trimester of pregnancy (carbimazole teratogenic) and thyroid storm
Definitive: radioiodine (I-131) — first-line definitive in non-pregnant adults; total thyroidectomy if large goitre, compressive symptoms, or patient declines radioiodine
traps: Carbimazole agranulocytosis: warn all patients to stop carbimazole and seek urgent FBC if they develop sore throat or fever — rare but life-threatening; occurs in first 3 months
Propylthiouracil (not carbimazole) in first trimester: carbimazole is teratogenic (aplasia cutis, choanal atresia) — switch to PTU in T1, then reassess; PTU has risk of hepatotoxicity

Thyroid storm: precipitated by surgery, infection, or iodine load in uncontrolled thyrotoxicosis — treat with high-dose PTU, Lugol's iodine (given 1 hour after PTU), IV beta-blocker, dexamethasone, and supportive care
Isotope scan distinction: do not treat with radioiodine without confirming diffuse (Graves) vs. nodular uptake — toxic MNG and solitary adenoma have different prognosis and RAI dosing

name: Hypoglycaemia
presentation: Autonomic: sweating, tremor, palpitations, hunger, anxiety (adrenergic/cholinergic surge, BM typically <4 mmol/L)
Neuroglycopenic: confusion, drowsiness, slurred speech, focal weakness, seizure, coma (BM typically <3 mmol/L)
Headache and blurred vision (early neuroglycopenic)
Nocturnal hypoglycaemia: nightmares, morning headache, unrefreshed sleep
Recurrent episodes without autonomic warning = hypoglycaemia unawareness (autonomic neuropathy or prior episodes blunting response)
signs: Diaphoresis and pallor (sympathoadrenal activation)
Tachycardia and hypertension (catecholamine surge)
Confusion, agitation or reduced GCS (neuroglycopenia)
Focal neurological deficit or seizure (mimics stroke — always check BM)

bedside: Capillary blood glucose (BM): confirms hypoglycaemia at <4 mmol/L; <3 mmol/L in non-diabetic requires formal venous sample
Obs: HR, BP (tachycardia, hypertension common); GCS to guide route of treatment
bloods: Venous glucose: send simultaneously with insulin/C-peptide at the time of hypoglycaemia (Whipple's triad requires documented low glucose)
Insulin level: elevated (>3 mIU/L) when glucose <3 mmol/L indicates endogenous or exogenous insulin excess
C-peptide: elevated = endogenous hyperinsulinism (insulinoma, sulphonylurea); suppressed = exogenous insulin injection
Sulphonylurea screen (plasma/urine): distinguishes sulphonylurea-induced hyperinsulinism from insulinoma when C-peptide is raised
Cortisol and IGF-II if non-islet tumour hypoglycaemia suspected; TFTs if hypothyroidism a contributor
imaging: CT pancreas (triple-phase): first-line to localise insulinoma (>90% sensitivity for lesions >1 cm)
MRI pancreas or endoscopic ultrasound (EUS): if CT negative — EUS most sensitive for small insulinomas
Selective arterial calcium stimulation with hepatic venous sampling: used pre-operatively when cross-sectional imaging is inconclusive

mx: Conscious and able to swallow: oral fast-acting carbohydrate (glucose tablets, Lucozade) — 'rule of 15' approach; repeat BM in 15 min and recheck
Unconscious or unsafe swallow: IV glucose (dextrose bolus) first-line in hospital; IM glucagon (1 mg) if no IV access — acts by stimulating hepatic glycogenolysis
Insulinoma: surgical resection (enucleation or pancreatectomy); diazoxide (inhibits insulin secretion) as bridge or if inoperable
traps: Glucagon fails in starvation, alcohol excess, and liver disease — hepatic glycogen stores depleted, so IM glucagon will not work; IV dextrose is the only reliable option

Whipple's triad must be satisfied before labelling a patient as having pathological hypoglycaemia: (1) symptoms consistent with hypoglycaemia, (2) documented low plasma glucose, (3) symptoms relieved by glucose correction
Exogenous insulin injection produces high insulin but suppressed C-peptide — raised insulin + suppressed C-peptide = factitious hypoglycaemia (Munchausen) until proven otherwise
Focal neurology or seizure with no obvious cause — always check BM immediately; hypoglycaemia mimics TIA/stroke and is fully reversible if treated promptly

name: Hypothyroidism
presentation: Fatigue and lethargy
Cold intolerance
Weight gain despite poor appetite
Constipation
Menorrhagia (primary hypothyroidism)
signs: Goitre (in Hashimoto's or iodine deficiency)
Slow-relaxing (hung-up) reflexes
Bradycardia
Dry, coarse skin and hair loss
Periorbital oedema / non-pitting oedema (myxoedema)

bedside: Obs: bradycardia, hypothermia, raised BP (diastolic hypertension)
ECG: sinus bradycardia, low-voltage complexes, prolonged QT interval
Urine dipstick: exclude other causes of oedema
bloods: Primary hypothyroidism: TSH elevated, free T4 low
Secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary failure): TSH low or inappropriately normal, free T4 low
Subclinical hypothyroidism: TSH elevated, free T4 normal
TPO antibodies: positive in Hashimoto's thyroiditis
FBC: normocytic or macrocytic anaemia; lipids: hypercholesterolaemia; CK: elevated (myopathy)
imaging: Thyroid USS: assess goitre size, nodularity; Hashimoto's gives heterogeneous hypoechoic appearance
CXR: cardiomegaly (pericardial effusion in severe/myxoedema)

mx: Levothyroxine (T4 replacement) — start low, titrate to TSH in normal range
Start at low dose in elderly or ischaemic heart disease (risk of precipitating angina/MI); increase slowly
Monitor TFTs at 6–8 weeks after dose change; annual TFTs once stable
Pregnancy: increase levothyroxine dose by ~25–30% as soon as pregnancy confirmed; TSH target <2.5 mU/L
traps: Starting full-dose levothyroxine in elderly or cardiac patients can precipitate angina or MI — always start low and go slow
Secondary hypothyroidism (low TSH + low T4): exclude pituitary disease and replace cortisol BEFORE levothyroxine (risk of adrenal crisis if cortisol-deficient)
Myxoedema coma: rare but life-threatening — hypothermia, reduced GCS, hypoventilation; needs IV T3/T4, IV hydrocortisone, ITU
Amiodarone causes both hypo- and hyperthyroidism; TFTs must be interpreted with free T4 as TSH may be unreliable

name: Type 2 diabetes
presentation: Polyuria and polydipsia (osmotic diuresis from glucosuria)
Fatigue and lethargy
Recurrent infections (UTIs, candidiasis, skin infections)
Blurred vision (osmotic lens changes)
Incidental hyperglycaemia on routine bloods
signs: Peripheral neuropathy — glove-and-stocking sensory loss, reduced vibration sense and ankle reflexes
Diabetic retinopathy — background (dot haemorrhages, hard exudates) or proliferative (neovascularisation) on fundoscopy
Foot signs — Charcot foot deformity, neuropathic/ischaemic ulceration, absent pedal pulses
Acanthosis nigricans — velvety hyperpigmentation at neck/axillae (insulin resistance marker)

bedside: Capillary blood glucose (BM) — random ≥11.1 mmol/L with symptoms is diagnostic
Urine dipstick — glycosuria; also check for proteinuria (nephropathy screening)
Blood pressure — hypertension common co-morbidity; target <130/80 mmHg in T2DM
bloods: Fasting plasma glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L on two occasions (or once with symptoms) — diagnostic
HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol (6.5%) on two occasions (or once with symptoms) — diagnostic; reflects 3-month average glucose
Random plasma glucose ≥11.1 mmol/L with classic osmotic symptoms — diagnostic without repeat
eGFR and urine albumin:creatinine ratio (ACR) — baseline renal function; ACR >3 mg/mmol = microalbuminuria
imaging: Fundoscopy / digital retinal photography — annual diabetic eye screening (detect retinopathy)
Renal ultrasound — if proteinuria or declining eGFR to exclude structural renal disease
Ankle-brachial pressure index (ABPI) — if peripheral arterial disease suspected (non-healing ulcer, absent pulses)

mx: Lifestyle modification first — dietary change (low glycaemic index, caloric deficit), weight loss, and increased physical activity; can achieve remission in obesity-related T2DM
Metformin (biguanide) — first-line oral agent; reduces hepatic gluconeogenesis; renal-dose-adjust, hold if eGFR <30
SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. empagliflozin/dapagliflozin) — add if established cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or CKD; cardio- and nephroprotective independently of glucose lowering
GLP-1 receptor agonist (e.g. semaglutide) — add if obesity prominent or CVD risk high; promotes weight loss and glucose-dependent insulin secretion
traps: MODY (Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young) — monogenic diabetes in thin young patients with strong family history; misclassified as T2DM; does not respond to metformin, responds to sulphonylureas (MODY3)
HbA1c unreliable in haemolytic anaemia, haemoglobinopathies (e.g. sickle cell, HbS/C), iron deficiency, and recent blood transfusion — use fasting glucose instead for diagnosis
SGLT2 inhibitors are indicated for renal and cardiovascular protection regardless of HbA1c target — do not withhold simply because glucose is controlled
Diagnosing T2DM requires two abnormal results in an asymptomatic patient; a single test suffices only when classic osmotic symptoms are present

name: Bacterial meningitis
presentation: Triad: severe headache, fever, neck stiffness
Non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash (meningococcal septicaemia)
Photophobia and phonophobia
Altered consciousness / confusion (reduced GCS)
Preceding URTI or close-contact exposure
signs: Kernig's sign — pain/resistance on knee extension with hip flexed at 90°
Brudzinski's sign — involuntary hip/knee flexion on passive neck flexion
Non-blanching petechiae or purpura (fails glass test)
Reduced GCS; may have focal neurology or papilloedema if raised ICP

bedside: Observations: fever, tachycardia, hypotension (sepsis)
Glass test: press glass on rash — non-blanching = meningococcal emergency
GCS assessment: guide LP safety and severity
bloods: FBC: raised WBC (neutrophilia in bacterial)
CRP: markedly elevated
Blood cultures: before antibiotics if no delay; identify organism
Meningococcal/pneumococcal PCR: blood or CSF; positive even post-antibiotics
Coagulation, U&E, glucose (paired glucose needed for CSF interpretation)
imaging: CT head before LP if: focal neurology, papilloedema, seizures, GCS <13, immunocompromised — to exclude raised ICP / mass lesion
LP: CSF appearance — turbid/cloudy (bacterial); clear (viral); xanthochromic (SAH)
CSF findings in bacterial meningitis: raised protein (>1 g/L), very low glucose (<50% serum), neutrophilia (>1000 cells/mm³), positive Gram stain/culture

mx: IV ceftriaxone immediately — do not delay for CT or LP; add IV amoxicillin if age <3 months or >60 years (Listeria cover)
IV dexamethasone — give before or with first antibiotic dose to reduce neurological sequelae (especially in pneumococcal disease)
Public health notification — meningococcal disease is notifiable; close contacts receive prophylactic rifampicin or ciprofloxacin
traps: Never delay antibiotics for CT or LP — treat immediately if meningitis suspected; investigations follow treatment
Listeria cover: add amoxicillin at age extremes (<3 months, >60 years) and in immunocompromised patients — ceftriaxone alone is insufficient
LP is contraindicated if focal neurology, papilloedema, seizures or GCS <13 — CT head first to exclude raised ICP
Non-blanching rash is a red flag even if other features are mild — assume meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise

name: Ischaemic stroke
presentation: Sudden-onset focal neurological deficit — FAST: facial droop, arm weakness, speech disturbance
MCA: contralateral hemiplegia/hemisensory loss (arm > leg), aphasia (dominant), neglect (non-dominant), homonymous hemianopia
ACA: contralateral leg > arm weakness, frontal lobe features (abulia, grasp reflex)
PCA: homonymous hemianopia ± cortical blindness, alexia without agraphia
