Posterior fossa (PICA/basilar): vertigo, ataxia, diplopia, dysphagia, crossed cranial nerve signs (ipsilateral face, contralateral body)
signs: Upper motor neurone pattern: contralateral spastic hemiparesis, hyperreflexia, upgoing plantar (Babinski)
Contralateral hemisensory loss (pain, temperature, proprioception depending on territory)
Contralateral homonymous hemianopia — assess by confrontation
Left-sided spatial neglect (non-dominant MCA): inattention to left hemispace on double simultaneous stimulation

bedside: Obs: BP (hypertension common acutely — do not lower unless >185/110 and thrombolysing), O2 sats, temperature
BM: exclude hypoglycaemia (mimics stroke; treat if <4 mmol/L)
ECG: identify AF (cardioembolic source); 12-lead also screens for concurrent ACS
NIHSS score: quantify deficit severity; guides thrombolysis eligibility
bloods: FBC: baseline; thrombocytopenia is contraindication to thrombolysis
Coagulation (PT/APTT/INR): anticoagulant use is contraindication to thrombolysis
Glucose and HbA1c: exclude hypoglycaemia; identify diabetes as risk factor
Fasting lipids: LDL for secondary prevention (statin therapy)
imaging: Non-contrast CT head (immediate): excludes haemorrhage before thrombolysis — ischaemic stroke appears NORMAL in first 6–24h; early signs: loss of grey–white differentiation, hyperdense MCA sign, sulcal effacement
MRI DWI (within 6h, or up to 24h with perfusion mismatch): shows restricted diffusion (bright DWI, dark ADC) within minutes of onset — far more sensitive than CT for acute ischaemia
CT angiography (CTA): identifies large vessel occlusion (LVO) amenable to mechanical thrombectomy

mx: Thrombolysis (alteplase IV): within 4.5h of onset if no contraindications (haemorrhage, BP >185/110 uncontrolled, anticoagulation, recent surgery, INR >1.7, glucose <2.7 or >22); BP must be controlled to ≤185/110 before administering
Mechanical thrombectomy: for LVO (M1/M2, basilar) within 6h (up to 24h with perfusion imaging selection) — superior to thrombolysis alone for proximal occlusions
Antiplatelet therapy: aspirin 300mg PO/PR within 24h of onset (delay 24h if thrombolysed); dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days in minor stroke/TIA (POINT/CHANCE criteria)
AF: anticoagulation (DOAC preferred over warfarin) started at 4–14 days post-stroke depending on infarct size; not acutely due to haemorrhagic transformation risk
traps: CT normal in first 6–24h — a normal CT does NOT exclude ischaemic stroke; it excludes haemorrhage; request MRI DWI if clinical suspicion high
Do NOT lower BP acutely unless >185/110 and planning thrombolysis, or hypertensive emergency — permissive hypertension maintains penumbral perfusion

Posterior circulation strokes mimic labyrinthitis/BPPV — vertigo + ataxia + any cranial nerve sign = posterior fossa stroke until proven otherwise (HINTS exam); CT posterior fossa has poor sensitivity
Hypoglycaemia mimics stroke exactly — always check BM first; correct before imaging
Anticoagulation timing in AF: starting too early risks haemorrhagic transformation; standard is 4–14 day rule based on infarct size (small = 4 days, large = 14 days)

name: Migraine
presentation: Unilateral throbbing/pulsating headache lasting 4–72 hours
Nausea/vomiting; aggravated by routine physical activity
Photophobia and phonophobia (often both)
Aura in ~30%: visual (fortification spectra, scotoma), sensory (unilateral paraesthesia), or speech disturbance — precedes headache by 20–60 min
Prodrome hours before: yawning, fatigue, food cravings; postdrome fatigue after
signs: Neurological examination typically normal between and during attacks
Fundoscopy normal — performed to exclude papilloedema (raised ICP)
Blood pressure measured to exclude hypertensive headache (>180/120 mmHg)
Neck stiffness absent — presence mandates exclusion of meningitis/SAH

bedside: Clinical diagnosis — no investigation required if classic history and normal exam
Blood pressure and pulse (exclude hypertensive emergency, exclude vasospasm)
Urine dipstick if first severe headache in young woman (exclude pre-eclampsia)
bloods: FBC, CRP, ESR if age >50 or new headache pattern — exclude giant cell arteritis (ESR >50 mm/hr supports GCA)
Glucose if aura present — exclude hypoglycaemia mimicking neurological aura
Coagulation/group-and-save only if CT-negative thunderclap headache proceeding to LP
imaging: CT head (non-contrast) only if red flags present: thunderclap onset, first/worst headache, progressive over weeks, focal neurology, papilloedema, immunocompromised, age >50 — normal CT does not exclude SAH (sensitivity ~98% at <6 h, falls thereafter)
MRI brain with gadolinium if CT negative but suspicion remains, or for haemiplegic/basilar migraine variants to exclude structural cause
LP (not imaging) for xanthochromia if thunderclap headache with normal CT — SAH excluded only by CSF analysis >12 h after onset

mx: Acute attack: oral triptan (5-HT1 agonist, e.g. sumatriptan) + NSAID (e.g. ibuprofen) or paracetamol; add antiemetic (e.g. metoclopramide) if vomiting; take early in attack

Prophylaxis (≥4 attacks/month or significant disability): first-line propranolol (beta-blocker) or topiramate (anticonvulsant); second-line amitriptyline (TCA) or candesartan; CGRP monoclonal antibodies (e.g. erenumab) for refractory cases
Non-pharmacological: identify and avoid triggers (sleep, diet, stress, hormonal), menstrual migraine — consider NSAID peri-menstrually or continuous OCP
traps: Thunderclap headache (maximal intensity at onset, 'worst ever') = SAH until proven otherwise — do not diagnose migraine without CT then LP
Topiramate is teratogenic — must not be used in women of childbearing potential without effective contraception; valproate similarly contraindicated
Triptans are contraindicated in ischaemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, and previous stroke/TIA — always check cardiovascular history before prescribing
Medication overuse headache: analgesics or triptans on ≥10–15 days/month paradoxically worsen headache frequency — the treatment is gradual withdrawal, not dose escalation

name: Parkinson's disease
presentation: Resting tremor — typically unilateral onset, 4–6 Hz, pill-rolling
Bradykinesia — slowness of movement, reduced amplitude on repetitive tasks
Rigidity — lead-pipe or cogwheel (tremor superimposed on rigidity)
Postural instability — falls, impaired righting reflexes (later feature)
Non-motor symptoms — anosmia, REM sleep behaviour disorder, constipation, depression, cognitive impairment
signs: Pill-rolling resting tremor — suppressed by voluntary movement, re-emerges at rest
Cogwheel rigidity — felt at wrist on passive flexion/extension; lead-pipe if no tremor component
Festinant gait — shuffling, small steps, stooped posture, reduced arm swing, difficulty initiating
Hypomimia — reduced facial expression; also micrographia, hypophonia

bedside: Clinical diagnosis — UK Brain Bank criteria: bradykinesia + at least one of rigidity, resting tremor, postural instability
Postural BP (lying and standing) — autonomic dysfunction; >20 mmHg systolic drop
Montreal Cognitive Assessment (MoCA) — baseline cognitive screen
bloods: Serum ceruloplasmin + copper + 24 h urine copper — exclude Wilson's disease in onset <50 years
TFTs — hypothyroidism can cause slowness and tremor mimicking Parkinsonism
FBC, renal and liver function — baseline before starting medications
imaging: DaTscan (123I-ioflupane SPECT) — images dopamine transporter density in striatum; reduced uptake confirms dopaminergic deficit, distinguishing Parkinson's from essential tremor (normal DaT) or drug-induced Parkinsonism (normal DaT); does not distinguish PD from atypical Parkinsonism (MSA, PSP)
MRI brain — not diagnostic for PD; used to exclude structural causes (hydrocephalus, vascular Parkinsonism, space-occupying lesion) or atypical features

mx: Levodopa (+ dopa-decarboxylase inhibitor, e.g. co-careldopa/co-beneldopa) — most effective motor therapy; first-line in older patients or those with significant disability
Dopamine agonists (e.g. ropinirole, pramipexole, rotigotine patch) — consider first-line in younger patients to delay levodopa motor complications; monitor for impulse control disorders
MAO-B inhibitors (e.g. rasagiline, selegiline) — mild symptoms or adjunct; neuroprotective claim unproven
traps: Never stop levodopa abruptly — risk of neuroleptic malignant syndrome-like akinetic crisis
Avoid dopamine-blocking antiemetics — metoclopramide and prochlorperazine worsen Parkinsonism; use domperidone (peripherally acting) instead
Dopamine agonist impulse control disorders — gambling, hypersexuality, binge eating; must counsel patient and carer before starting
Drug-induced Parkinsonism mimics PD — caused by antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine; DaTscan is normal, distinguishing it from true PD

name: Status epilepticus
presentation: Seizure lasting >5 minutes without spontaneous cessation
Recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness between episodes
Known epilepsy with subtherapeutic AED levels or missed doses
Acute precipitant: hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia, CNS infection, head injury, stroke, alcohol withdrawal
No return to baseline between episodes (distinguishes from cluster seizures)
signs: Active tonic-clonic activity or subtle motor signs (non-convulsive status)
Post-ictal confusion, reduced GCS, unresponsive between convulsions
Todd's paresis — transient focal weakness after cessation indicating focal cortical origin
Lateral tongue biting, urinary incontinence, cyanosis during ictal phase

bedside: BM — exclude hypoglycaemia as reversible cause (treat if <4 mmol/L)
Obs including temperature — pyrexia suggests CNS infection or systemic cause
12-lead ECG — exclude cardiac arrhythmia mimicking or precipitating event
bloods: Glucose and U&Es — hypo/hyperglycaemia, hyponatraemia, hypomagnesaemia as direct causes
FBC and CRP — infection screen; leucocytosis may be post-ictal or infective
AED drug levels (e.g. phenytoin, valproate) — subtherapeutic level common precipitant
Toxicology screen — alcohol, recreational drugs, tricyclics; ABG for metabolic acidosis and oxygenation
imaging: CT head — once stabilised; exclude structural cause (tumour, haemorrhage, abscess)
MRI brain with contrast — preferred if CT negative and no clear cause identified
EEG — essential if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected (no motor activity but altered consciousness)

mx: Airway, breathing, circulation — high-flow O2, IV access, position (recovery if possible), call for help
First-line benzodiazepine: IV lorazepam (preferred if IV access) or buccal midazolam / PR diazepam (if no IV); may repeat once after 5-10 minutes if seizure continues
Second-line (if seizure continues after 2 benzodiazepine doses): IV levetiracetam, sodium valproate, or phenytoin infusion — senior/anaesthetic involvement
Refractory status (>45 min or failed 2nd-line): rapid sequence induction and general anaesthesia in ITU
traps: Giving more than two benzodiazepine doses without escalating to second-line risks respiratory depression and delay — must escalate after max 2 doses
Missing hypoglycaemia as a correctable cause — always check BM immediately and treat before or alongside AEDs
Non-convulsive status epilepticus — patient appears confused or unresponsive without obvious motor activity; requires EEG to diagnose and is frequently missed
Phenytoin is contraindicated in absence and myoclonic status — know which AED to choose based on seizure type

name: TIA
presentation: Transient focal neurological deficit fully resolved within 24h (usually <1h)
Amaurosis fugax — sudden painless monocular visual loss ('curtain descending'), carotid territory
Contralateral hemiparesis or hemisensory loss, dysphasia, homonymous hemianopia
Vertebrobasilar: diplopia, dysphagia, ataxia, bilateral limb weakness or vertigo with focal signs
By definition: no residual deficit at time of assessment
signs: Neurological examination often entirely normal at presentation (deficit resolved)
Carotid bruit on auscultation — suggests ipsilateral carotid stenosis
Irregular pulse — AF as cardioembolic cause
Hypertensive retinopathy or Hollenhorst plaques on fundoscopy

bedside: ECG — AF (irregularly irregular, no P waves) as cardioembolic source
BM — exclude hypoglycaemia mimicking focal deficit
BP both arms — hypertension as risk factor; significant difference suggests subclavian steal
bloods: FBC — polycythaemia (raised Hb/haematocrit) increases thrombotic risk; thrombocytosis
Lipid profile — LDL elevation guides statin therapy
HbA1c and fasting glucose — diabetes as vascular risk factor
Clotting screen and thrombophilia screen — if young or no obvious cause
ESR/CRP — giant cell arteritis if >50y with temporal symptoms
imaging: MRI brain with DWI — may show restricted diffusion (small infarct) even if symptoms resolved; sequences to exclude haemorrhage and mimics
Carotid Doppler USS — stenosis >50% in symptomatic carotid territory indicates endarterectomy
CT brain — if MRI unavailable or contraindicated; excludes haemorrhage but less sensitive for ischaemia
Echocardiogram — if cardioembolic source suspected (AF, valvular disease, patent foramen ovale)

mx: Aspirin 300mg immediately (loading dose) then dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days, then clopidogrel monotherapy
Urgent specialist (TIA clinic or stroke unit) review within 24h of symptom onset — same-day if high-risk (ABCD2 ≥4, crescendo TIA, AF, known carotid stenosis)
Anticoagulate if AF identified — DOAC (e.g. apixaban) preferred over warfarin; do not delay for 2-week observation window
Statin therapy (high-intensity, e.g. atorvastatin) and antihypertensive if indicated
Carotid endarterectomy within 2 weeks if symptomatic carotid stenosis 50–99% (NASCET criteria)
traps: TIA + AF: start anticoagulation promptly — do NOT apply the 2-week antiplatelet-first rule used after established ischaemic stroke; the haemorrhagic transformation risk window does not apply to TIA
ABCD2 score is de-emphasised in current NICE guidance — do not use it to triage who gets urgent assessment; all TIAs warrant same-day or next-day specialist review
Amaurosis fugax = carotid territory TIA (ophthalmic artery branch of ICA) — triggers carotid Doppler and same-day review, not 'ophthalmology referral only'
Hypoglycaemia is the must-not-miss mimic — always check BM before labelling focal deficit as TIA/stroke
Crescendo TIA (≥2 TIAs in 1 week) = stroke emergency — admit immediately, do not discharge from ED

name: Acute kidney injury
presentation: Oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr) or anuria
Fluid overload: breathlessness, peripheral oedema, orthopnoea
Uraemic symptoms: nausea, vomiting, confusion, pruritus, hiccups
Precipitant history: dehydration, sepsis, nephrotoxic drugs, urinary symptoms suggesting obstruction
signs: Fluid status: dry (pre-renal) — reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes, low BP, tachycardia; or overloaded — raised JVP, peripheral oedema, pulmonary crackles
Uraemic signs: pericardial friction rub, encephalopathy, asterixis (metabolic flap)
Obstruction signs: palpable bladder (urinary retention), renal angle tenderness
Sepsis signs: fever, hypotension, tachycardia as precipitating cause

bedside: Urine dipstick: haematuria/proteinuria suggests intrinsic renal cause (glomerulonephritis, vasculitis); nitrites/leucocytes suggest UTI/sepsis
Strict fluid balance and urine output monitoring (catheterise if oliguric)
ECG: peaked T waves, wide QRS, sine wave pattern — hyperkalaemia requires urgent management
bloods: Creatinine: KDIGO staging — Stage 1: ×1.5–1.9 baseline or rise ≥26 µmol/L in 48 h; Stage 2: ×2–2.9 baseline; Stage 3: ×3 baseline or ≥354 µmol/L or RRT required
Urea: elevated (urea:creatinine ratio >100:1 favours pre-renal); urea raised disproportionately in dehydration/GI bleed
Potassium: hyperkalaemia (>6.5 mmol/L or ECG changes) is a dialysis indication
Bicarbonate/ABG: metabolic acidosis (low HCO3, low pH) — severity guides need for RRT
FBC, CRP, blood cultures if sepsis; urine MC&S; immunology (ANCA, anti-GBM, complement) if intrinsic renal disease suspected
imaging: Renal USS: hydronephrosis = post-renal obstruction → urgent urology; bilateral small kidneys (<9 cm) = underlying CKD not AKI
CXR: pulmonary oedema (fluid overload), cardiomegaly; assess for infective precipitant

mx: Identify and treat cause: pre-renal (IV fluids — crystalloid bolus, then reassess), intrinsic renal (stop nephrotoxics, treat underlying cause e.g. immunosuppression for vasculitis), post-renal (catheterise for retention; urology for obstruction)
Stop nephrotoxics — DAMN: Diuretics (in hypovolaemia), ACEi/ARBs, Metformin, NSAIDs; review all drug doses for eGFR

Manage complications: treat hyperkalaemia (calcium gluconate, insulin-dextrose, salbutamol); correct acidosis; restrict fluid if overloaded; renal replacement therapy (RRT) indications — AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolyte disturbance, Intoxication, Overload, Uraemia
traps: DAMN drugs: must stop Diuretics (if pre-renal), ACEi/ARBs, Metformin (lactic acidosis risk), NSAIDs — a common AKT question asks which to withhold

Dialysis indications — AEIOU: refractory Acidosis (pH <7.1), Electrolyte disturbance (K+ >6.5 unresponsive to treatment), Intoxication (lithium, salicylates), fluid Overload refractory to diuretics, Uraemia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding)
Small kidneys on USS (<9 cm bilaterally) = CKD not AKI — chronicity changes management entirely; do not aggressively fluid-load CKD
Urea:creatinine ratio >100:1 suggests pre-renal AKI (dehydration, cardiac failure, hepatorenal) but is also elevated in GI bleed and high protein intake — not diagnostic alone

name: CKD
presentation: Often asymptomatic in early stages (G1-G3a) — detected incidentally on bloods or urine dip
Fatigue, dyspnoea, pallor (anaemia of CKD)
Nocturia, frothy urine (proteinuria), peripheral oedema
Uraemic symptoms (G4-G5): anorexia, nausea, pruritus, confusion, pericarditis
signs: Pallor (normochromic normocytic anaemia of CKD)
Hypertension (cause and consequence of CKD)
Peripheral oedema (nephrotic-range proteinuria or fluid overload in late CKD)
AV fistula scar/thrill at wrist or antecubital fossa (established on haemodialysis)
Uraemic frost — white crystalline deposits on skin (rare, end-stage)

bedside: Urine dip: proteinuria (marker of glomerular damage), haematuria (glomerulonephritis, ADPKD)
Urine ACR (albumin:creatinine ratio): A1 <3, A2 3-30, A3 >30 mg/mmol — staging and risk stratification
BP measurement: target <130/80 in CKD with proteinuria
bloods: eGFR (CKD-EPI): G1 ≥90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15 ml/min/1.73m² — diagnosis requires <60 for >3 months OR structural/functional kidney damage
FBC: normochromic normocytic anaemia (reduced erythropoietin production)
U&E: raised urea and creatinine; hyperkalaemia (especially G4-G5 or on ACEi/ARB)
Bone profile: raised phosphate, low calcium, raised PTH, low vitamin D (renal osteodystrophy/secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Bicarbonate: metabolic acidosis (reduced H+ excretion in advanced CKD)
imaging: Renal USS: small (<9 cm), echogenic kidneys with cortical thinning bilaterally — distinguishes CKD from AKI (normal/large kidneys in AKI)
USS also excludes obstructive uropathy (hydronephrosis — reversible cause of CKD)
ADPKD exception: large kidneys bilaterally with multiple cysts on USS

mx: Renoprotection: ACEi (e.g. ramipril) or ARB (e.g. losartan) — first-line if proteinuria present; reduce intraglomerular pressure and proteinuria; SGLT2 inhibitor (e.g. dapagliflozin) added for diabetic CKD or CKD with proteinuria
BP control: target <130/80 mmHg (tighter if proteinuria); ACEi/ARB preferred agent

Treat complications: EPO analogue (erythropoiesis-stimulating agent) for anaemia of CKD once iron replete; phosphate binders + active vitamin D (alfacalcidol) for renal bone disease; sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis
Statin for all CKD patients (high cardiovascular risk regardless of lipid levels)
RRT planning (haemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, transplant) when approaching G5 or uraemic symptoms
traps: ACEi/ARB: acceptable to continue if creatinine rises ≤25% or eGFR falls ≤25% from baseline — this is expected haemodynamic effect, not toxicity; stop if K⁺ exceeds 6.0 mmol/L
Small echogenic kidneys = CKD (chronic scarring/fibrosis); normal or large kidneys in AKI — USS is key to differentiate when chronicity is uncertain
CKD requires eGFR <60 for >3 months OR evidence of kidney damage (proteinuria, haematuria, structural abnormality) — a single low eGFR reading alone is not CKD
Anaemia of CKD is normochromic normocytic with low reticulocyte count — do not assume iron deficiency without checking ferritin/transferrin saturation; treat iron deficiency first before EPO
ADPKD causes large not small kidneys — do not be caught out by bilateral flank masses in a young patient with family history and haematuria
Metformin and NSAIDs should be withheld/avoided in G4-G5 CKD — metformin (lactic acidosis risk), NSAIDs (further reduce GFR via afferent arteriolar constriction)

name: UTI
presentation: Dysuria, frequency, urgency (lower UTI / cystitis)
Haematuria — macroscopic or dipstick
Suprapubic pain or discomfort
Pyelonephritis: loin pain, fever, rigors, nausea/vomiting (systemic sepsis possible)
Asymptomatic bacteriuria — significant only in pregnancy
signs: Suprapubic tenderness (cystitis)
Renal angle / costovertebral angle tenderness (pyelonephritis)
Fever, tachycardia, hypotension if urosepsis
No signs in uncomplicated lower UTI

bedside: Urine dipstick: nitrites + leucocyte esterase positive (nitrites alone highly specific for bacteriuria)
Urine MC&S (midstream sample) — send before antibiotics if pyelonephritis, men, pregnant, recurrent or treatment failure
Pregnancy test in all women of childbearing age
Observations / NEWS if systemically unwell (sepsis 6 if urosepsis)
bloods: FBC: raised WBC (neutrophilia) in pyelonephritis/urosepsis
CRP: elevated in upper UTI
U&E and creatinine: renal function if pyelonephritis or obstruction suspected
Blood cultures x2 if febrile / urosepsis before antibiotics
imaging: Renal USS: first-line if obstruction suspected, recurrent UTI, men, or inadequate treatment response
CT KUB (non-contrast): if calculi or anatomical abnormality suspected
CT urogram / MAG3 renogram: recurrent upper UTI or structural anomaly work-up

mx: Lower UTI (non-pregnant): nitrofurantoin 3–7 days (first-line) or trimethoprim 7 days (if low resistance risk)
Pyelonephritis (oral, well): ciprofloxacin 7 days or co-amoxiclav 14 days; guided by MC&S
Pyelonephritis (IV, unwell/urosepsis): IV co-amoxiclav or gentamicin; step down to oral once apyrexial 24h
Pregnancy: treat all bacteriuria (including asymptomatic); cefalexin or nitrofurantoin (avoid nitrofurantoin at term ≥36 weeks); avoid trimethoprim in 1st trimester (folate antagonist)
Catheter-associated UTI: treat only if symptomatic; change catheter before starting antibiotics
traps: Always treat bacteriuria in pregnancy — asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy causes pyelonephritis and preterm labour if untreated
Avoid trimethoprim in 1st trimester (folate antagonist → neural tube defect risk) and avoid nitrofurantoin at term (≥36 weeks — neonatal haemolysis risk)
Catheter UTI: do NOT treat asymptomatic bacteriuria in catheterised patients — treat only if symptomatic (fever, rigors, loin pain, delirium)
Nitrites negative does not exclude UTI — Enterococcus and Staphylococcus saprophyticus do not produce nitrites; rely on leucocyte esterase + clinical context
Men with UTI need investigation (USS ± urology referral) — anatomical abnormality or prostatitis must be excluded

name: Iron deficiency anaemia
presentation: Fatigue and lethargy
Dyspnoea on exertion
Pica (craving for ice, dirt, or clay)
Restless legs syndrome
Angular cheilitis and sore mouth
signs: Pallor (conjunctival, palmar)
Koilonychia (spoon-shaped nails)
Angular cheilitis
Atrophic glossitis (smooth, sore tongue)

bedside: Observations (HR↑ if severe — compensatory tachycardia)
Urine dip (haematuria as bleeding source)
Stool faecal occult blood (if GI blood loss suspected)
bloods: FBC: Hb↓, MCV↓ (microcytic), MCH↓ (hypochromic)
Blood film: microcytic hypochromic red cells, pencil cells, anisocytosis, poikilocytosis
Serum ferritin↓ (most sensitive single test for iron deficiency)
Serum iron↓, TIBC↑, transferrin saturation↓
imaging: OGD ± colonoscopy to identify GI bleeding source (mandatory if unexplained IDA in adults)
Coeliac serology (anti-tTG IgA) — malabsorption cause
CT abdomen/pelvis if 2WW colorectal cancer referral indicated

mx: Oral ferrous sulfate (iron replacement — continue for 3 months after Hb normalises to replenish stores)
Investigate and treat the underlying cause (do not simply replace iron without finding the source)
2-week-wait urgent referral if unexplained IDA in patient ≥60 years (colorectal cancer until proven otherwise)
traps: Always find the cause — treating IDA without identifying the source misses GI malignancy
Continue iron 3 months after Hb normalises — stopping at Hb correction leaves stores depleted and anaemia recurs
Ferritin is an acute phase reactant — can be falsely normal or elevated in infection/inflammation despite iron deficiency; check CRP alongside
Thalassaemia trait also gives microcytic hypochromic picture — distinguish by iron studies (ferritin normal/raised) and blood film (target cells, basophilic stippling)

name: Neutropenic sepsis
presentation: Fever ≥38°C (or hypothermia <36°C) in patient on chemotherapy
Rigors, sweating, feeling unwell — often no localising source
Fatigue, confusion or altered consciousness in severe cases
Recent chemotherapy (typically 7–14 days prior — nadir period)
May present with diarrhoea, mucositis, or mouth pain (portal of entry)
signs: Signs often blunted — no pus, minimal erythema due to absent neutrophils
Fever (or rarely hypothermia); tachycardia ± hypotension (septic shock)
Inspect mouth (mucositis, oral candida), central line/port sites (erythema, tenderness)
Perianal area may be source — inspect carefully; no rectal examination

bedside: Observations: temp >38°C or <36°C, HR >90, RR >20, BP (MAP <65 = septic shock)
UKONS risk score (≥3 = high risk; admission + IV antibiotics)
Urine dipstick ± MSU if urinary symptoms
bloods: FBC: neutrophils <0.5 ×10⁹/L (severe) or <1.0 ×10⁹/L with falling trend — defines neutropenia
Blood cultures: two sets — peripheral vein AND central line/port (before antibiotics but do not delay Abx)
CRP, U&E, LFTs, lactate (lactate ≥2 = sepsis; ≥4 = septic shock), coagulation screen
Procalcitonin if available; G-CSF level not routinely measured
imaging: CXR: exclude pneumonia (infiltrates may be subtle without neutrophils)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: if no source identified and patient deteriorating — neutropenic enterocolitis (typhlitis)
USS or CT of suspected collection/line site if clinically indicated

mx: Piperacillin-tazobactam (Tazocin) IV — broad-spectrum anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam; first-line per UKONS/NICE NG151
Start IV antibiotics within 1 hour of presentation — do not wait for culture results or neutrophil count confirmation
Add vancomycin (glycopeptide) if MRSA risk, line infection suspected, or haemodynamically unstable; G-CSF (filgrastim) if prolonged neutropenia or high-risk features
traps: DO NOT wait for neutrophil count result or culture results before giving antibiotics — 1-hour rule is absolute
Signs are blunted: absence of erythema, fluctuance or pus does NOT exclude serious infection — threshold to treat must be low
Afebrile patients can still have neutropenic sepsis — hypothermia or just feeling unwell post-chemo counts; don't anchor on fever
Always take cultures from BOTH peripheral vein AND central line before antibiotics — tells you if line is the source — but never delay Abx to do this

name: VTE (DVT/PE)
presentation: DVT: unilateral calf swelling, pain, warmth, erythema, pitting oedema
PE: sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, collapse/syncope
PE: tachycardia, low O2 sats, sense of impending doom
signs: DVT: unilateral calf swelling >3 cm vs contralateral, pitting oedema, warmth, erythema; Homan's sign unreliable (insensitive and non-specific)
PE: tachycardia (most common), tachypnoea, hypoxia, pleural rub, raised JVP (RV strain)
Massive PE: hypotension, shocked, RV heave, loud P2

bedside: Obs + O2 sats: tachycardia, hypoxia (SpO2 <94%)
ECG: sinus tachycardia most common; S1Q3T3 (deep S in I, Q wave + T inversion in III) suggests RV strain; new RBBB
Wells score pre-test probability (DVT and PE versions) — guides D-dimer vs direct imaging
bloods: D-dimer: ELISA — high sensitivity, low specificity; use only if Wells low/intermediate probability to exclude VTE; raised in infection, malignancy, pregnancy (not useful to confirm)
ABG: Type 1 respiratory failure (low PaO2, low/normal PaCO2) due to V/Q mismatch; respiratory alkalosis from hyperventilation
FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting: baseline before anticoagulation; consider thrombophilia screen if unprovoked (defer until off anticoagulation)
imaging: CTPA (CT pulmonary angiogram): first-line for suspected PE — filling defects in pulmonary arteries; saddle embolus in massive PE
V/Q scan: alternative if CTPA contraindicated (contrast allergy, renal impairment, pregnancy — reduces radiation to breasts)
USS lower limb (Doppler): first-line for DVT — non-compressible vein on compression; CXR may show Westermark sign (oligaemia), Hampton's hump (peripheral wedge opacity), or may be normal

mx: DOAC first-line (apixaban or rivaroxaban) — no bridging required; provoked VTE: 3 months; unprovoked or high-risk: 6 months+
LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin) in pregnancy (DOACs cross placenta — contraindicated), active cancer, or antiphospholipid syndrome (use warfarin, target INR 2-3 or 3-4 if arterial)
Massive PE with haemodynamic instability: systemic thrombolysis (alteplase) or surgical embolectomy; catheter-directed thrombolysis if available
traps: DOACs contraindicated in pregnancy — use LMWH throughout; switch to LMWH or heparin around delivery
Antiphospholipid syndrome: DOACs inferior to warfarin — use warfarin (INR 2-3); triple-positive APS warrants lifelong anticoagulation
Provoked (transient risk factor e.g. surgery, immobility) = 3 months; unprovoked = minimum 6 months, consider indefinite with shared decision-making
D-dimer is only useful to exclude VTE in low/intermediate Wells probability — never use to confirm diagnosis; always raised in pregnancy, malignancy, post-op

name: Warfarin reversal / supratherapeutic INR
presentation: Incidental supratherapeutic INR on routine monitoring (no bleeding)
Easy bruising or prolonged bleeding from minor cuts
Frank haematuria, haemoptysis, or rectal bleeding
Intracranial bleed: sudden headache, focal neurology, reduced GCS
Drug interaction or intercurrent illness causing INR to rise unexpectedly
signs: Visible bruising, petechiae, or haematoma at venepuncture/pressure sites
Bleeding from gums, epistaxis, or operative wounds
Focal neurological deficit or reduced consciousness (intracranial bleed)
Haemodynamic instability (tachycardia, hypotension) suggesting major haemorrhage

bedside: Observations: HR, BP, O2 sats — haemodynamic stability determines urgency
Urine dipstick: haematuria
Point-of-care INR (finger-prick) if available for rapid result
bloods: INR: guides management — >5 with no bleed, >8, or major bleed each have distinct thresholds
FBC: haemoglobin to quantify blood loss; platelets to exclude concurrent thrombocytopaenia
U&E and LFTs: renal/hepatic causes of warfarin sensitivity
Group and save / crossmatch if major haemorrhage suspected
imaging: CT head (non-contrast) urgently if any head injury, headache, or neurological signs — exclude intracranial haemorrhage
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if internal haemorrhage suspected and haemodynamically stable
Ultrasound: retroperitoneal or soft-tissue haematoma if clinically indicated

mx: Major bleed (any INR): stop warfarin immediately; IV vitamin K 5–10 mg; Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC, e.g. Beriplex) for rapid factor replacement; involve haematology and consider urgent reversal of anticoagulation
INR >8, no bleeding (or minor bleeding): stop warfarin; oral vitamin K 1–5 mg; recheck INR in 24 hours; restart warfarin at lower dose once INR in range
INR 5–8, no bleeding: withhold 1–2 doses of warfarin; recheck INR; consider low-dose oral vitamin K if other bleeding risk factors present
traps: PCC (Beriplex) preferred over FFP for major bleeding: faster reversal, lower volume, more complete factor replacement — FFP is no longer first-line
Common drug interactions raising INR: antibiotics (metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, fluconazole), amiodarone, NSAIDs, cranberry juice — always take a drug/diet history
Do not give high-dose IV vitamin K if restarting anticoagulation is planned — will cause warfarin resistance for days; use low oral dose instead
Intracranial haemorrhage on warfarin requires immediate reversal regardless of how supratherapeutic the INR is — do not wait for CT result before giving PCC

name: Cellulitis
presentation: Spreading erythema with well-demarcated or ill-defined border
Local warmth, swelling, and tenderness
Pain — often disproportionate pain should raise suspicion of necrotising fasciitis
Systemic features if severe: fever, rigors, tachycardia, hypotension
Portal of entry identifiable in ~50%: skin break, tinea pedis, insect bite, leg ulcer
signs: Eron class I: no systemic features, no comorbidities; II: systemic features OR significant comorbidity; III: systemic illness (e.g. confusion, tachycardia) OR limb-threatening; IV: sepsis/life-threatening
Mark erythema border with skin marker pen and time/date to track progression
Lymphangitis: red streaking tracking proximally along lymphatic channels
Portal of entry: interdigital tinea, wound, ulcer, puncture — look actively

bedside: Obs: temperature, HR, BP, RR, SpO2 — assess for sepsis (Eron III-IV)
Mark erythema border with pen + timestamp to monitor spread or resolution
Urine dip/MRSA swab of portal of entry if recurrent or hospital-acquired
bloods: FBC: raised WBC (neutrophilia) indicates bacterial infection
CRP/ESR: elevated — useful to trend response to treatment
Blood cultures: if systemic features (fever, rigors, Eron III-IV) before starting antibiotics
U&Es and LFTs: baseline before IV antibiotics; renal function guides dosing
imaging: USS soft tissue: exclude underlying abscess (needs incision and drainage) or fluid collection
MRI soft tissue: if necrotising fasciitis suspected — tracking along fascial planes; do not delay surgery for imaging if clinical suspicion high
Plain X-ray: if gas-forming infection or foreign body suspected

mx: Eron I-II (no systemic features): oral flucloxacillin (covers Staph aureus and Streptococcus) — first-line; clarithromycin or co-amoxiclav if penicillin allergic or polymicrobial
Eron III-IV (systemic illness): IV flucloxacillin; add benzylpenicillin if extensive streptococcal spread; escalate to piperacillin-tazobactam if MRSA risk or severe
Elevate affected limb; mark border and reassess at 24-48 h; treat underlying predisposing factors (e.g. tinea pedis with topical antifungal)
traps: Necrotising fasciitis red flags: wooden-hard induration, pain out of proportion to appearance, skin necrosis/blistering, crepitus (gas) = surgical emergency — immediate surgical debridement, do not await imaging if clinically suspected
Bilateral lower limb 'cellulitis' is almost never bilateral cellulitis — find the true cause: venous insufficiency/lipodermatosclerosis, lymphoedema, contact dermatitis
DVT must be excluded in unilateral leg swelling with erythema — USS Doppler if clinical doubt; cellulitis and DVT can co-exist
Abscess within cellulitis: fluctuance or failure to respond to antibiotics → USS and incision + drainage required; antibiotics alone will not clear a pus collection

name: Sepsis
presentation: Fever >38°C or hypothermia <36°C, often with rigors
Confusion or altered consciousness (new)
Tachycardia and tachypnoea
Hypotension (SBP <90 or drop >40 from baseline)
Source symptoms: dysuria/loin pain (urine), productive cough/pleurisy (chest), abdominal pain/peritonism (abdo), skin erythema/warmth (cellulitis)
signs: SIRS criteria: HR >90, RR >20, temp <36 or >38°C, WBC <4 or >12
End-organ dysfunction: mottled/clammy skin, capillary refill >2s, oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/hr), GCS drop
Hypotension refractory to fluids suggests septic shock
Source of infection: chest crepitations, suprapubic/renal angle tenderness, wound/line site erythema

bedside: Observations + NEWS2 score (trigger for sepsis pathway if ≥5)
Point-of-care lactate (target <2 mmol/L; ≥2 = severe sepsis, ≥4 = very high risk)
Urine output monitoring (catheterise if shocked) + BM
bloods: Blood cultures ×2 sets (before antibiotics — but do not delay antibiotics >1 hr)
Lactate: ≥2 mmol/L = sepsis with organ dysfunction; ≥4 = septic shock regardless of BP
FBC (WBC raised or low; neutrophilia common), CRP/procalcitonin (infection marker)
U&E/creatinine (AKI), LFTs, clotting (DIC screen), glucose, ABG (metabolic acidosis)
imaging: CXR (pneumonia, effusion, pulmonary oedema)
USS abdomen/pelvis (abscess, biliary obstruction, collections)
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if source remains unclear after initial assessment

mx: Sepsis Six — within 1 hour: GIVE O2 (target SpO2 >94%), IV fluids (500 mL crystalloid bolus), IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (per local policy, e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam)
Sepsis Six — TAKE: blood cultures (×2 before antibiotics), serum lactate, urine output (catheterise if shocked)
Septic shock: vasopressors (noradrenaline) if MAP <65 despite adequate fluid resuscitation; escalate to ITU; source control (drain abscess, remove infected line)
traps: Take cultures BEFORE antibiotics — but never delay antibiotics beyond 1 hour waiting for cultures; the cultures come first only if it takes <15 minutes
Lactate ≥2 mmol/L = severe sepsis even with normal BP; lactate ≥4 = very high mortality risk regardless of haemodynamic status
Septic shock definition: sepsis + vasopressor required to maintain MAP ≥65 + lactate ≥2 despite adequate fluids — not simply 'low BP'
Hypothermia (<36°C) is a SIRS criterion and can indicate sepsis in frail/elderly — do not be reassured by absence of fever

name: Giant cell arteritis
presentation: New-onset headache in patient >50 years (typically temporal)
Scalp tenderness (e.g. pain on combing hair)
Jaw claudication (pain on chewing — highly specific)
Visual disturbance: amaurosis fugax, diplopia, or sudden painless monocular visual loss
PMR features: bilateral shoulder/hip girdle stiffness and aching
signs: Tender, thickened, nodular or pulseless temporal artery on palpation
Reduced visual acuity or visual field defect (afferent pupillary defect if optic nerve involved)
Fundoscopy: pale swollen optic disc with blurred margins (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy)
Fundoscopy: central retinal artery occlusion appearance (pale retina, cherry-red spot)

bedside: Visual acuity (Snellen chart) — document before starting steroids
Fundoscopy: anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (pale swollen disc) or central retinal artery occlusion
bloods: ESR: markedly elevated, often >50 mm/hr (may be >100)
CRP: elevated (sensitive marker, may be raised when ESR is normal)
FBC: normocytic normochromic anaemia of chronic disease; thrombocytosis
Plasma viscosity (PV): elevated; LFTs (ALP raised in ~30%)
imaging: Temporal artery biopsy: giant cell granulomatous inflammation with intimal hyperplasia — skip lesions mean a long segment (>1 cm) must be sampled
USS temporal artery: hypoechoic halo sign (mural oedema) — non-invasive first-line in many centres
MRI/CT angiography: if large-vessel GCA suspected (aortitis, subclavian involvement)

mx: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone) IMMEDIATELY on clinical suspicion — do not wait for biopsy result; delay risks irreversible blindness
If visual loss already present: IV methylprednisolone (high-dose pulsed corticosteroids) before switching to oral
Bone protection: bisphosphonate + calcium/vitamin D supplementation from outset given prolonged steroid course
traps: Do NOT delay steroids to await biopsy — permanent bilateral blindness can occur within hours; treat on clinical suspicion
Biopsy remains positive for up to 2 weeks after starting steroids due to skip lesions — still worth doing
Normal ESR does not exclude GCA — CRP and clinical features can be positive with normal ESR (~5% of cases)
Jaw claudication (not jaw pain at rest) is highly specific for GCA and should prompt urgent treatment

name: Gout - acute
presentation: Acute monoarthritis — typically 1st metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint (podagra)
Rapid onset: maximal pain within 12–24 hours, often wakes patient at night
Severe pain, swelling, erythema, warmth — joint may be exquisitely tender to light touch
Precipitants: alcohol (especially beer), dehydration, diuretics (thiazide/loop), dietary purine load, illness/surgery
Background risk factors: male sex, obesity, chronic kidney disease, cyclosporin use
signs: Erythema, warmth, and swelling over affected joint — may mimic cellulitis
Exquisite tenderness on palpation or passive movement of joint
Tophi: firm subcutaneous deposits at ear helix, olecranon, Achilles tendon — sign of chronic tophaceous gout
Fever may be present — can cause diagnostic confusion with septic arthritis

bedside: Observations: temperature (fever common), HR, BP
Joint aspiration (gold standard): needle-shaped negatively birefringent crystals under polarised light microscopy; also send for MC&S to exclude septic arthritis
bloods: Serum urate: often normal or LOW during acute attack — do not use to rule out gout acutely; baseline useful once resolved
WBC and CRP: raised — confirms inflammatory/infective process
Renal function (U&E): CKD is a risk factor; affects choice of NSAIDs and allopurinol dosing
LFTs and glucose: baseline before starting NSAIDs or colchicine
imaging: X-ray (plain): may be normal in acute attack; chronic gout shows punched-out juxta-articular erosions with overhanging edge ('rat-bite' erosions), preserved joint space
Ultrasound: double contour sign — urate crystal deposition on articular cartilage surface; also detects tophi and joint effusion
Joint aspiration (diagnostic): monosodium urate crystals — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent (yellow parallel, blue perpendicular to compensator axis)

mx: Acute attack: NSAIDs (e.g. naproxen) first-line if no contraindication; colchicine is an alternative (SE: diarrhoea, nausea); short-course oral corticosteroid if both NSAIDs and colchicine contraindicated (e.g. severe CKD)
Rest and elevate affected joint; ice packs for symptomatic relief
Do NOT start urate-lowering therapy (allopurinol/febuxostat) during an acute attack — precipitates prolonged or recurrent attacks; begin 4–6 weeks after resolution with prophylactic colchicine cover
traps: Serum urate can be NORMAL or LOW during the acute attack — a normal result does not exclude gout; recheck 4–6 weeks later
Never start allopurinol during an acute attack — it mobilises urate and worsens or prolongs the flare; always wait until fully resolved
Colchicine causes dose-dependent GI toxicity (diarrhoea, vomiting) — a common exam side-effect question; reduce dose in renal impairment
Septic arthritis must be excluded — both cause acute hot swollen joint with fever; joint aspiration MC&S is mandatory if diagnosis unclear (septic arthritis is a surgical emergency)

name: Gout - chronic/prevention
presentation: Recurrent acute attacks (≥2/year) — trigger for urate-lowering therapy
Tophi: firm, chalky deposits at ear helix, olecranon bursa, Achilles tendon
Chronic tophaceous arthritis — joint deformity, chronic pain between attacks
Uric acid renal stones (radiolucent on plain X-ray)
Renal impairment from urate nephropathy
signs: Tophi — ear helix, olecranon, Achilles tendon, finger pulps
Asymmetric joint deformity (1st MTP, midfoot, ankle, knee)
Skin ulceration/discharge of chalky material over tophus
Signs of chronic renal disease (hypertension, pallor) if urate nephropathy

bedside: Urine dipstick — haematuria/proteinuria if renal involvement; uric acid stones cause haematuria
Joint aspiration during attack — needle-shaped, negatively birefringent monosodium urate crystals under polarised light
bloods: Serum urate — target <360 μmol/L on urate-lowering therapy (or <300 μmol/L if tophi present); note: can be normal during acute attack
U&E and eGFR — renal impairment is both a cause (reduced urate excretion) and consequence of hyperuricaemia; dose-adjust allopurinol
FBC — exclude haematological cause of high cell turnover (e.g. myeloproliferative disorder)
Fasting glucose and lipids — gout associated with metabolic syndrome
imaging: X-ray affected joint — chronic changes: well-defined punched-out erosions with overhanging cortical edge (rat-bite erosion), preserved joint space until late, soft-tissue tophi
Ultrasound — double-contour sign (urate crystals on articular cartilage), hyperechoic tophaceous deposits
DECT (dual-energy CT) — identifies urate deposits if diagnosis uncertain; not routine

mx: Start urate-lowering therapy 2–4 weeks after acute attack settles (not during attack — risks prolonging it); first-line: allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor)
Always co-prescribe prophylactic cover when initiating allopurinol — colchicine (preferred) or NSAID for minimum 3–6 months to prevent initiation flare
Titrate allopurinol dose to serum urate <360 μmol/L; lifestyle: low-purine diet, reduce alcohol (especially beer), avoid diuretics where possible, ensure adequate hydration
traps: Never start allopurinol during an acute attack — it precipitates a flare; wait 2–4 weeks and always co-prescribe colchicine/NSAID cover for first 3–6 months

Allopurinol + azathioprine = dangerous interaction — allopurinol inhibits xanthine oxidase (the enzyme that metabolises azathioprine/6-MP), causing azathioprine toxicity (severe myelosuppression); combination is contraindicated or requires 75% dose reduction of azathioprine
Serum urate can be normal or low during an acute attack — do not use a normal result to exclude gout acutely; repeat 4–6 weeks after attack
HLA-B*5801 screening before allopurinol in Han Chinese, Thai, Korean patients — associated with severe cutaneous reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome)

name: Rheumatoid arthritis
presentation: Symmetrical small joint polyarthritis (MCPs, PIPs — spares DIPs)
Morning stiffness >1 hour, improves with activity
Systemic features: fatigue, weight loss, low-grade fever
Extra-articular: rheumatoid nodules, sicca symptoms, pleuritis, episcleritis
Insidious onset over weeks–months in a woman aged 30–50
signs: MCP/PIP swelling, tenderness, warmth; DIP spared
Ulnar deviation of fingers at MCPs
Boutonniere deformity (PIP flexion, DIP hyperextension) or swan-neck deformity (PIP hyperextension, DIP flexion)
Wasting of dorsal interossei; rheumatoid nodules over pressure points (olecranon)

bedside: Joint count (28-joint DAS28): swollen + tender joint tally
Obs: fever suggests septic arthritis or flare
Urine dip: exclude renal involvement (vasculitis)
bloods: Rheumatoid factor (RF): positive in ~70% — not specific (also SLE, Sjogren's, infection)
Anti-CCP antibodies: >95% specific for RA, positive before symptoms; titre predicts erosive disease
CRP + ESR: raised — track disease activity and treatment response
FBC: normocytic anaemia of chronic disease; thrombocytosis in active disease
LFTs + U&E + FBC: baseline before starting methotrexate (and monitored regularly)
imaging: X-ray hands + feet (PA): periarticular osteoporosis → joint space narrowing → marginal erosions (bare areas at joint margins) → subluxation/deformity (late)
Ultrasound joints: synovitis confirmed by power Doppler; detects erosions earlier than plain X-ray
MRI hands: most sensitive for early bone erosions and synovitis; used when X-ray normal but clinical suspicion high

mx: DMARD monotherapy first-line: methotrexate (weekly oral/SC) + folic acid 5mg once weekly (different day) to reduce toxicity
Bridging: short-course prednisolone or IM methylprednisolone to suppress active inflammation while DMARD takes effect (6–12 weeks onset)
Escalation: combination csDMARDs (add hydroxychloroquine ± sulfasalazine) or switch to biologic (anti-TNF e.g. etanercept/adalimumab) if DAS28 remains >3.2 after 6 months adequate DMARD
traps: Methotrexate is WEEKLY not daily — daily dosing causes fatal bone marrow suppression and mucositis; always prescribe as 'once weekly'
Folic acid must be co-prescribed (different day to methotrexate) — omitting it causes avoidable toxicity
Trimethoprim (and co-trimoxazole) is contraindicated with methotrexate — both are folate antagonists; combination risks life-threatening pancytopenia
Effective contraception required for both sexes on methotrexate — teratogenic; stop ≥3 months before planned conception

name: Bacterial vaginosis / Trichomonas
presentation: BV: thin grey-white vaginal discharge with fishy (amine) odour, worse after sex/menstruation
BV: no itch, no soreness — absence of inflammation is characteristic
Trich: frothy yellow-green discharge with offensive odour
Trich: vulval pruritus, dysuria, dyspareunia
signs: BV: homogeneous grey-white discharge coating vaginal walls, pH >4.5, no erythema or inflammation
Trich: strawberry cervix (colpitis macularis) — punctate haemorrhages on cervix
Trich: frothy offensive discharge, vulval and vaginal erythema
Trich: may be asymptomatic in up to 50% of women

bedside: Vaginal pH: >4.5 in both BV and Trich (normal <4.5)
Whiff test (KOH/amine test): fishy odour positive in BV; may be positive in Trich
Amsel's criteria for BV (3 of 4): homogeneous grey discharge, pH >4.5, positive whiff test, clue cells on wet prep
bloods: STI screen if Trich confirmed (Trich is an STI — partner notification and treatment required)
Consider HIV, hepatitis B/C, syphilis, gonorrhoea, chlamydia screen alongside Trich
imaging: Wet prep microscopy (saline): clue cells (epithelial cells studded with Gardnerella) — diagnostic of BV
Wet prep microscopy (saline): motile flagellated trichomonads — diagnostic of Trich
NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test): gold standard for Trichomonas vaginalis detection

mx: Both BV and Trich: oral metronidazole (nitroimidazole) — first-line for both
Trich: treat sexual partner(s) and advise abstinence until both complete treatment (Trich is an STI)
BV: partner treatment not routinely recommended (BV is not classified as an STI)
traps: BV is NOT an STI — no routine partner treatment; Trich IS an STI — always treat partner
Metronidazole + alcohol = disulfiram-like reaction (flushing, vomiting) — counsel patients to avoid alcohol during and 48 hours after course
BV in pregnancy increases risk of preterm labour, late miscarriage, and postpartum endometritis — screen and treat
Trich can be asymptomatic — do not exclude based on absence of symptoms alone

name: Eclampsia
presentation: Tonic-clonic seizure in pregnancy (>20 weeks) or up to 4 weeks postpartum
Often preceded by pre-eclampsia features: headache, visual disturbance, epigastric/RUQ pain
Sudden-onset altered consciousness or coma post-ictally
Hypertension and proteinuria preceding seizure
signs: Tonic-clonic seizure (may be witnessed)
Hypertension (BP ≥140/90, often severe)
Proteinuria on urine dip (≥2+)
Peripheral or facial oedema
Reduced GCS / post-ictal confusion

bedside: BP (serial monitoring — establish severity)
Urine dip (proteinuria ≥2+) then MSU/PCR
CTG (fetal heart rate monitoring for fetal distress)
bloods: FBC — thrombocytopenia in HELLP
LFTs — raised transaminases in HELLP; RUQ pain marker
LDH + blood film — haemolysis screen (HELLP)
Clotting (PT/APTT/fibrinogen) — DIC risk
U&E + creatinine — renal impairment
Urate — elevated in pre-eclampsia/eclampsia
imaging: CT head (non-contrast) — exclude intracranial haemorrhage or other cause if atypical
MRI brain — posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES) pattern if available
USS fetal wellbeing + liquor volume + umbilical artery Dopplers

mx: Seizure control: IV/IM magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) — loading dose then maintenance infusion; continue 24 hours after last seizure or delivery
Blood pressure control: IV labetalol or hydralazine for acute severe hypertension (target <150/100); oral nifedipine once stable
Deliver once mother stabilised — definitive treatment; mode depends on gestation and clinical status
traps: MgSO4 is anticonvulsant not antihypertensive — a separate agent is needed for BP control
Mg toxicity signs: loss of patellar reflexes (first sign), respiratory depression, cardiac arrest — monitor reflexes hourly
Mg toxicity antidote: IV calcium gluconate 10 ml of 10% solution
Eclampsia can occur up to 4 weeks postpartum — seizure in a recently-delivered patient is eclampsia until proven otherwise
Continue MgSO4 for 24 hours after the last seizure or after delivery, whichever is later

name: Ectopic pregnancy
presentation: Amenorrhoea (typically 6–8 weeks)
Unilateral lower abdominal/pelvic pain
Vaginal bleeding (usually light, dark)
Shoulder tip pain (diaphragmatic irritation from haemoperitoneum — ruptured)
Collapse/syncope (ruptured)
signs: Adnexal tenderness (unilateral)
Cervical excitation (pathognomonic of pelvic pathology)
Peritonism — guarding, rigidity (ruptured)
Haemodynamic instability — tachycardia, hypotension (ruptured)

bedside: Urine βhCG: positive (confirms pregnancy)
Observations: tachycardia + hypotension = ruptured until proven otherwise
bloods: Serum quantitative βhCG: discriminatory zone 1500–2000 IU/L (no IUP on TVUSS above this = ectopic until proven otherwise)
Serial βhCG (48 h): normal IUP rises ≥63%; plateau/rise <63% or fall suggests ectopic/failed pregnancy
FBC: haemoglobin (haemorrhage)
Group & crossmatch: prepare for possible surgery
imaging: TVUSS (first-line): empty uterus + adnexal mass ± fetal heartbeat; free fluid in pouch of Douglas indicates haemoperitoneum
No intrauterine pregnancy seen when βhCG >1500 IU/L = ectopic until proven otherwise

mx: Expectant: small, asymptomatic, βhCG falling (<1000 IU/L and declining) — close outpatient surveillance
Medical: methotrexate (folate antagonist) — criteria: haemodynamically stable, mass <35 mm, no fetal heartbeat, βhCG <1500 IU/L, no contraindications (renal/hepatic disease, immunodeficiency)
Surgical: salpingectomy (preferred) or salpingotomy — indicated if ruptured, haemodynamically unstable, medical criteria not met, or methotrexate fails; laparoscopic approach unless too unstable
traps: Ruptured ectopic = surgical emergency — do not delay for further investigations if haemodynamically unstable
Heterotopic pregnancy (simultaneous IUP + ectopic) — rare spontaneously but increased risk after IVF; finding an IUP on USS does not exclude ectopic
Avoid pregnancy for 3 months after methotrexate (teratogenic — folate antagonist)
βhCG below discriminatory zone does not exclude ectopic — repeat serial levels and USS; a very early IUP may not yet be visible

name: Gestational diabetes
presentation: Often asymptomatic — screen-detected on OGTT
Polyuria and polydipsia if markedly hyperglycaemic
Risk factors: BMI >30, previous GDM, macrosomic baby (>4.5 kg) in prior pregnancy, first-degree relative with T2DM, South Asian / Black / Middle Eastern ethnicity
signs: Uterus large-for-dates on symphysis-fundal height measurement
Macrosomia on abdominal palpation (large fetal parts, ballotable head above pelvic brim at term)
Polyhydramnios — uterus tense, fluid thrill, fetal parts difficult to feel
USS: abdominal circumference >95th centile, increased liquor volume

bedside: Capillary blood glucose (BM) monitoring — fasting and 1 h post-meal targets (fasting <5.3, 1 h <7.8 mmol/L)
Urine dipstick for glycosuria — poor screening tool (low sensitivity/specificity in pregnancy); persistent 2+ warrants OGTT
bloods: 75 g OGTT (diagnostic): fasting glucose ≥5.6 mmol/L OR 2-hour glucose ≥7.8 mmol/L = GDM (NICE NG3)
HbA1c — NOT used to diagnose or monitor GDM (red cell turnover increased in pregnancy; falsely low)
Booking HbA1c or fasting glucose if high risk — used to detect pre-existing T2DM undiagnosed before pregnancy (HbA1c ≥48 mmol/mol = T2DM)
imaging: USS growth scan (28, 32, 36 weeks): abdominal circumference, estimated fetal weight, liquor volume (AFI/deepest pool)
Polyhydramnios: deepest vertical pool >8 cm or AFI >24 cm — associated with poor glycaemic control
Umbilical artery Doppler if growth restriction suspected (placental insufficiency co-exists occasionally)

mx: Step 1 — diet and exercise: low glycaemic index diet, 30 min moderate activity daily; review at 1–2 weeks
Step 2 — metformin: if glucose targets not met on lifestyle alone (fasting <5.3, 1 h post-meal <7.8 mmol/L)
Step 3 — insulin: add (or use instead of metformin if contraindicated/not tolerated) if targets still not met; also start insulin immediately if fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L at diagnosis
Postnatal: stop all hypoglycaemic agents at delivery; retest with fasting glucose or HbA1c at 6–13 weeks postnatally
traps: HbA1c is unreliable in pregnancy — increased red cell turnover causes falsely low result; OGTT is the diagnostic test
OGTT thresholds in GDM (NICE): fasting ≥5.6 OR 2 h ≥7.8 — lower than standard T2DM thresholds; do not confuse
Fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L at OGTT = start insulin immediately (do not trial diet/metformin first)
GDM resolves after delivery but confers ~50% lifetime risk of T2DM — postnatal retest and annual fasting glucose thereafter is mandatory
GDM is a risk factor for shoulder dystocia, stillbirth, neonatal hypoglycaemia, and pre-eclampsia — not purely a maternal metabolic issue

name: Postpartum haemorrhage
presentation: Primary PPH: blood loss >500 ml after vaginal delivery or >1000 ml after caesarean section within 24 hours
4 T's aetiology: Tone (uterine atony — 70-80% of PPH), Trauma (lacerations, uterine rupture), Tissue (retained placenta/membranes), Thrombin (coagulopathy)
Haemodynamic instability: tachycardia, hypotension, pallor, cold clammy peripheries
Uterine atony: fundus palpably soft and boggy post-delivery, fails to contract
Secondary PPH: excessive bleeding 24 hours to 12 weeks postpartum — consider retained products or endometritis
signs: Boggy, poorly contracted uterus on abdominal palpation (atony)
Haemodynamic compromise: shock index (HR/SBP) >1 suggests significant haemorrhage
Visible genital tract trauma on inspection (perineal/vaginal/cervical lacerations)
Failure of placenta to deliver or incomplete placenta on inspection (retained tissue)

bedside: Observations: shock index (HR/SBP) — >1 significant, >1.7 severe; continuous pulse oximetry and BP monitoring
Catheterise: urine output target >30 ml/hr as marker of renal perfusion and volume status
Clinical estimate of blood loss: weigh swabs, measure collected blood; note underestimation is common
bloods: FBC: haemoglobin trend (initial Hb may be falsely normal in acute haemorrhage) and platelet count
Clotting screen + fibrinogen: DIC develops in severe PPH — fibrinogen <2 g/L is critical threshold indicating consumption
Group and crossmatch: urgent sample for blood bank; activate major haemorrhage protocol if needed
Venous blood gas: lactate and base excess for real-time assessment of tissue perfusion and shock severity
imaging: USS uterus and pelvis: identify retained products of conception (echogenic material within uterine cavity)
Interventional radiology: uterine artery embolisation if bleeding refractory to medical management and patient haemodynamically stable enough

mx: Call for help + major haemorrhage protocol: 2 large-bore IV cannulae, IV fluid resuscitation, transfuse packed red cells:FFP:platelets in 1:1:1 ratio; tranexamic acid (antifibrinolytic) within 3 hours

Stepwise uterotonics for atony — uterine massage (bimanual compression) → oxytocin IV infusion (first-line uterotonic) → ergometrine IM/IV (ergot alkaloid, vasoconstriction + uterine contraction) → carboprost IM (prostaglandin F2α analogue) → misoprostol (prostaglandin E1, sublingual/rectal)

4 T's-targeted management: Tone → uterotonics/massage; Trauma → surgical repair of lacerations; Tissue → manual removal of placenta/ERPC; Thrombin → correct coagulopathy with FFP/cryoprecipitate/platelets
Escalation: intrauterine balloon tamponade → B-Lynch suture (compression suture) → uterine/internal iliac artery ligation → hysterectomy (definitive last resort)
traps: Carboprost (prostaglandin F2α) is CONTRAINDICATED in asthma — causes severe bronchospasm; use misoprostol instead
Ergometrine is CONTRAINDICATED in hypertension and pre-eclampsia/eclampsia — causes vasoconstriction and can precipitate hypertensive crisis or stroke
Do not rely on initial haemoglobin to exclude significant haemorrhage — Hb may be falsely normal in acute haemorrhage before haemodilution occurs; use shock index and clinical picture
Fibrinogen is a sensitive early marker of DIC in PPH — a falling fibrinogen (<2 g/L) mandates cryoprecipitate; standard clotting (PT/APTT) may be normal until late

name: Pre-eclampsia
presentation: Hypertension ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks gestation
Proteinuria (≥300 mg/24 h or PCR ≥30 mg/mmol)
Frontal headache (warning of impending eclampsia)
Visual disturbance: blurring, flashing lights, scotomata
Epigastric or RUQ pain (hepatic capsule stretch / HELLP)
signs: Elevated BP on repeated measurement (≥140/90 or severe ≥160/110)
Pitting oedema — hands, face, pre-tibial (non-specific but common)
Hyperreflexia and clonus (≥3 beats = severe — risk of seizure)
RUQ tenderness on palpation (hepatic involvement / HELLP)

bedside: Urine dipstick: proteinuria 2+ or more — quantify with PCR
Urine protein:creatinine ratio (PCR) ≥30 mg/mmol confirms significant proteinuria
BP measurement: two readings ≥140/90 mmHg ≥4 h apart
bloods: FBC: thrombocytopenia (platelets <100 ×10⁹/L in HELLP)
LFTs: elevated AST/ALT (≥2× upper limit); ↑bilirubin indicates haemolysis
LDH: ↑LDH (>600 U/L) — marker of haemolysis (H in HELLP)
U&E and creatinine: rising creatinine reflects renal impairment
Clotting / coagulation screen: DIC risk if HELLP severe
imaging: USS fetus + umbilical artery Dopplers: fetal growth restriction, oligohydramnios, absent/reversed end-diastolic flow
CT head: indicated after eclamptic seizure to exclude haemorrhage or other cause

mx: Antihypertensive: labetalol first-line (oral or IV); nifedipine MR alternative; target BP <150/100 mmHg
Seizure prevention: IV magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) if severe pre-eclampsia or eclampsia — loading dose then infusion
Delivery: definitive treatment — timing depends on gestation and severity; corticosteroids if <34 weeks for fetal lung maturity
Prophylaxis: low-dose aspirin from 12 weeks in women with ≥1 high-risk or ≥2 moderate-risk factors
traps: Mg toxicity sequence: loss of patellar reflexes → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest — monitor reflexes hourly; antidote is IV calcium gluconate
Delivery is the only cure — all other measures are temporising; do not delay delivery to chase a normal BP
HELLP can occur without severe hypertension or significant proteinuria — diagnose on bloods, not BP alone
Methyldopa: safe antenatally but avoid postnatally (risk of postnatal depression); switch to labetalol or nifedipine after delivery

name: Depression
presentation: Persistent low mood (≥2 weeks)
Anhedonia — loss of interest/pleasure in previously enjoyed activities
Anergia — fatigue or loss of energy
Biological symptoms: early morning wakening, diurnal mood variation (worse AM), psychomotor retardation/agitation, reduced appetite/weight loss, loss of libido
Suicidal ideation or thoughts of self-harm
signs: Psychomotor retardation or agitation on MSE
Flat or blunted affect; tearful
Poor eye contact, downcast gaze
Poverty of thought; slow, monosyllabic responses

bedside: PHQ-9: score 0–4 minimal, 5–9 mild, 10–14 moderate, 15–19 moderately severe, 20–27 severe
Mental state examination (MSE) — assess cognition, thought content, risk
Suicide risk assessment — ideation, intent, plan, means, protective factors
bloods: TFTs — exclude hypothyroidism as reversible organic cause
FBC — exclude anaemia
Serum calcium — hypercalcaemia can cause low mood
Fasting glucose — exclude diabetes-related mood disturbance
imaging: CT/MRI head only if late-onset (>60 yrs), atypical features, or cognitive impairment to exclude organic cause (e.g. space-occupying lesion, vascular dementia)

mx: Mild depression: active monitoring, guided self-help, or low-intensity CBT — antidepressants not first line
Moderate–severe depression: SSRI (sertraline first choice) + psychological therapy (CBT); review within 1–2 weeks of starting medication
Treatment-resistant or severe/psychotic depression: consider augmentation (add antipsychotic or lithium), or referral to secondary care psychiatry
traps: Review within 1 week if patient is <25 yrs or high suicide risk at initiation — SSRIs can transiently increase suicidality in early treatment
Never stop SSRIs abruptly — taper to avoid discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, electric-shock sensations, flu-like symptoms)
SSRI + NSAID (or aspirin) = significantly increased GI bleed risk — consider PPI cover
PHQ-9 ≥10 does not automatically mandate an SSRI — severity, patient preference, and safety must guide the decision

name: Generalised anxiety disorder
presentation: Persistent, excessive worry across multiple domains (work, health, finances) lasting >6 months
Difficult to control worry; patient recognises it as disproportionate
Somatic symptoms: palpitations, sweating, tremor, GI upset (nausea/diarrhoea)
Restlessness, fatigue, poor concentration, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance
signs: MSE: anxious or agitated affect, worry-focused thought content, preserved insight
Tachycardia and diaphoresis on autonomic examination
Hyperventilation (respiratory rate elevated, may cause tingling/tetany)
Tremor of outstretched hands; no thyroid goitre or exophthalmos (distinguishes from hyperthyroidism)

bedside: GAD-7 questionnaire: score ≥10 suggests moderate-severe GAD
Mental state examination (MSE): assess affect, thought content, cognition, insight
Urine dip and BP to exclude phaeochromocytoma if episodic hypertension
bloods: TFTs (TSH + free T4): hyperthyroidism is the key organic mimic — exclude before diagnosis
Fasting glucose: hypoglycaemia causes adrenergic symptoms mimicking anxiety
FBC + CRP: exclude anaemia (causes tachycardia/fatigue) and systemic illness
Caffeine and substance history (not a blood test but mandatory organic screen)
imaging: ECG: exclude arrhythmia (SVT, AF) in patients with prominent palpitations
24-hour Holter monitor if paroxysmal palpitations persist with normal resting ECG
Imaging has limited role in GAD; CT/MRI only if focal neurological signs present

mx: Stepped care (NICE): Step 1 — psychoeducation and active monitoring; Step 2 — low-intensity CBT or guided self-help; Step 3 — high-intensity CBT or SSRI (sertraline first-line) or SNRI (venlafaxine)
Pregabalin: second-line pharmacological option (licensed for GAD); monitor for dependence
Avoid benzodiazepines long-term — short-term crisis use only (≤2–4 weeks); risk of tolerance and dependence
traps: Always exclude hyperthyroidism (TSH) before diagnosing GAD — presentations overlap completely
Phaeochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension, sweating and palpitations — rare but dangerous to miss
Benzodiazepines are not first-line and must not be prescribed long-term; SSRI/CBT is the evidence-based approach
Pregabalin is second-line not first-line; also carries dependence potential and is a controlled drug (Schedule 3)

name: Serotonin syndrome
presentation: Triad: altered mental status (agitation, confusion, restlessness) + autonomic instability + neuromuscular abnormalities
Onset within hours (usually <24 h) of starting, increasing dose, or adding a serotonergic drug
Autonomic features: tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, mydriasis
Neuromuscular features: clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor, myoclonus
Severity spectrum: mild (tremor + tachycardia) → life-threatening (hyperthermia >41°C, rhabdomyolysis, seizures)
signs: Clonus — especially inducible ankle clonus and spontaneous clonus (pathognomonic feature in Hunter criteria)
Hyperreflexia — lower limbs > upper limbs
Diaphoresis and flushed skin
Tachycardia and hypertension on obs; hyperthermia (can be extreme)

bedside: Observations: temperature (fever), HR (tachycardia), BP (hypertension), SpO2
Medication review: identify all serotonergic agents (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, tramadol, linezolid, triptans, lithium, fentanyl, St John's Wort)

Hunter Criteria assessment: serotonergic drug + [spontaneous clonus / inducible clonus + agitation or diaphoresis / ocular clonus + agitation or diaphoresis / tremor + hyperreflexia / hypertonia + temp >38°C + ocular or inducible clonus]
bloods: CK — elevated in moderate-severe disease; markedly raised if rhabdomyolysis
U&E — AKI secondary to rhabdomyolysis; electrolyte derangement
FBC and glucose — baseline; exclude infection or hypoglycaemia as contributory cause
Clotting screen — if severe, exclude DIC
imaging: CT head — only to exclude intracranial cause (haemorrhage, encephalitis) if diagnosis uncertain or reduced GCS

mx: Stop all serotonergic drugs immediately
Supportive care: IV fluids, active cooling for hyperthermia, benzodiazepines (lorazepam/diazepam) for agitation and neuromuscular excitability
Cyproheptadine (serotonin antagonist) as antidote in moderate-severe cases; ITU for life-threatening hyperthermia or rhabdomyolysis
traps: Distinguishing from NMS: serotonin syndrome = rapid onset (hours), clonus/hyperreflexia, serotonergic drug cause; NMS = slow onset (days-weeks), lead-pipe rigidity, dopamine-blocking drug cause (antipsychotics)
Tramadol + SSRI is a classic precipitant — tramadol inhibits serotonin reuptake and is a common co-prescription
Linezolid (antibiotic) is an MAOI and is serotonergic — combined with SSRIs causes serotonin syndrome
Hunter criteria (not Sternbach) are the validated diagnostic tool — clonus is the key discriminating sign

name: Acne vulgaris
presentation: Comedones (open/closed), inflammatory papules, pustules, nodules or cysts on face, chest, and back
Onset typically puberty (12–18 yrs); can persist into adulthood, especially in women
Scarring (atrophic ice-pick/boxcar or hypertrophic keloid) with severe or inadequately treated disease
Significant psychological burden: low self-esteem, depression, social withdrawal — assess QoL (DLQI)
signs: Open comedones (blackheads) and closed comedones (whiteheads) — non-inflammatory lesions
Inflammatory papules (red, <5 mm) and pustules (pus-filled) overlying comedones
Nodules (≥5 mm, deep, tender) and cysts (fluctuant, risk of scarring) in severe disease
Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and atrophic or hypertrophic scarring; distribution: face (especially T-zone), upper chest, upper back

bedside: Clinical diagnosis — no routine investigations needed for typical presentation
Skin swabs (Gram stain + culture) only if atypical or treatment-resistant: exclude Gram-negative folliculitis (Klebsiella/Enterobacter) or Pityrosporum folliculitis
bloods: Pregnancy test (serum β-hCG) — mandatory before and monthly during isotretinoin (Pregnancy Prevention Programme)
LFTs (ALT/AST) — baseline and 1 month after starting isotretinoin; hepatotoxicity risk
Fasting lipid profile (total cholesterol, triglycerides) — baseline and monitoring during isotretinoin; hypertriglyceridaemia risk
FBC — baseline before isotretinoin; testosterone, DHEAS, LH:FSH, 17-OHP if hormonal acne suspected in women (PCOS, CAH)
imaging: Pelvic USS — if PCOS suspected (hormonal acne in women with irregular cycles, hirsutism)
No imaging required for routine acne diagnosis

mx: Mild (comedonal/few papulopustular): topical benzoyl peroxide (BPO) + topical retinoid (adapalene) ± topical antibiotic (clindamycin) — always pair antibiotic with BPO to limit resistance

Moderate (widespread papulopustular/mild nodular): add oral antibiotic (lymecycline or doxycycline) for maximum 3 months — never combine oral + topical antibiotic; always co-prescribe BPO; consider combined oral contraceptive (co-cyprindiol) in women

Severe/scarring/treatment-resistant: oral isotretinoin (vitamin A retinoid) — specialist-only prescribing, 16–24 week course; highly teratogenic — all female patients must be enrolled in Pregnancy Prevention Programme (2 forms of contraception, monthly pregnancy tests)
traps: Isotretinoin is highly teratogenic (Category X) — all women of childbearing potential must complete the Pregnancy Prevention Programme (2 contraceptive methods, monthly pregnancy tests, dispensed in 30-day supplies only); never prescribe if pregnancy test positive

Never prescribe oral antibiotic plus topical antibiotic together — promotes antibiotic resistance; always pair any antibiotic (oral or topical) with benzoyl peroxide which has anti-resistance activity
Oral antibiotics should not be used for more than 3 months — resistance risk; reassess at 3 months and step up to isotretinoin if inadequate response rather than continuing antibiotics

Isotretinoin side effects frequently tested: teratogenicity, dry lips/skin/eyes (most common — prescribe emollients), raised triglycerides, deranged LFTs, possible mood changes/depression (monitor), contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding

name: Eczema
presentation: Intense itch (cardinal symptom) — worse at night, disrupts sleep
Flexural distribution: antecubital/popliteal fossae, wrists, neck (adults); face/scalp/extensor surfaces (infants)
Triggers: soaps, detergents, heat, sweat, stress, certain fabrics
Personal or family history of atopy (asthma, hay fever, eczema)
Dry skin (xerosis) with episodic flares
signs: Erythematous, lichenified plaques in flexural sites; excoriation marks
Dennie-Morgan folds (infraorbital skin folds) and allergic shiners
Infected eczema: weeping, crusted, honey-coloured exudate; may have satellite lesions (S. aureus)
In chronic disease: post-inflammatory hypo- or hyperpigmentation; skin thickening

bedside: EASI or SCORAD severity score to guide treatment step
Skin swabs (MC&S) if secondary infection suspected — S. aureus commonest
Patch testing if allergic contact dermatitis suspected (distinct from atopic eczema)
bloods: Total IgE — elevated in atopic eczema
FBC — eosinophilia supports atopic disease
Specific IgE (RAST) — identify relevant aeroallergens or food allergens if history suggests
imaging: Skin biopsy only if diagnosis uncertain or malignancy (e.g. mycosis fungoides) needs exclusion

mx: Emollients: apply liberally and continuously (soap substitute + moisturiser) — cornerstone of all-step management
Topical corticosteroids: potency matched to severity and site (mild — hydrocortisone 1% for face; moderate — clobetasone; potent — betamethasone for lichenified plaques); use for flares only

Step up: topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/pimecrolimus) for sensitive sites or steroid-sparing; dupilumab (IL-4/IL-13 inhibitor) for moderate-severe refractory eczema; short-course oral prednisolone bridging if severe acute flare
traps: Eczema herpeticum: punched-out monomorphic vesicles/erosions on background eczema + systemic upset = dermatological emergency — admit, IV aciclovir, ophthalmology review if periorbital
Finger-tip unit (FTU) dosing: 1 FTU covers one hand area; failure to use adequate amounts of topical steroid is a common cause of inadequate control
Do not use potent steroids on the face or flexures long-term — atrophy, telangiectasia, striae risk
Steroid phobia in parents/patients leads to under-treatment; address directly and explain that short-course topical use at correct potency is safe

name: Anaphylaxis
presentation: Rapid onset (seconds–minutes) after trigger exposure (food, drug, insect sting)
Urticaria and/or angioedema — erythematous wheals, swelling of lips/tongue/throat
Airway compromise — stridor (upper), wheeze (lower), sensation of throat tightening
Circulatory collapse — hypotension, syncope, dizziness
GI symptoms — nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps (esp. food allergy)
signs: Urticaria — raised erythematous wheals, widespread
Angioedema — non-pitting swelling of face, lips, tongue, uvula
Stridor — inspiratory noise indicating upper airway obstruction
Wheeze — expiratory, indicating bronchospasm
Hypotension + tachycardia — distributive shock
Flushing and generalised erythema

bedside: Obs: continuous BP, HR, O2 sats, RR — clinical diagnosis; do not delay treatment for tests
Peak flow if able — bronchospasm severity
ECG — exclude arrhythmia if cardiovascular compromise
bloods: Mast cell tryptase (MCT): 3 samples — at presentation (0 h), 1–2 h post-reaction, and convalescent (>24 h or follow-up); rise confirms mast cell degranulation
FBC — eosinophilia may support allergic diathesis (not acute diagnostic)
U&E/glucose — baseline; hypoglycaemia as alternative cause of collapse
imaging: CXR — if aspiration suspected, significant bronchospasm, or failure to respond to treatment
Limited acute role — clinical diagnosis drives immediate management

mx: IM adrenaline 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) into anterolateral thigh — FIRST and most important step; repeat every 5 min if no improvement
High-flow O2 + large-bore IV access + IV crystalloid bolus for hypotension; lie flat with legs raised (avoid sitting up in shock)
Chlorphenamine (antihistamine) + hydrocortisone (steroid) as ADJUNCTS only — given after adrenaline, do not substitute for it; observe minimum 6–12 h for biphasic reaction
traps: Adrenaline route: IM anterolateral thigh (NOT IV except in cardiac arrest or by specialists with monitoring — IV causes fatal arrhythmias if given inappropriately)
Antihistamines and steroids are NOT acute life-saving treatments — adrenaline is; giving these instead of adrenaline delays definitive treatment
Sitting up a shocked patient can precipitate cardiac arrest — lie flat with legs elevated unless respiratory compromise forces upright posture
Biphasic (late) reaction occurs in up to 20% — observe for minimum 6–12 h; warn patient symptoms can recur up to 72 h later
Always prescribe self-injectable adrenaline (EpiPen) on discharge and refer to allergy clinic

name: Opioid overdose
presentation: Reduced consciousness or unresponsive
Slow or shallow respiratory rate (RR <12)
Pinpoint pupils (miosis)
Context: known opioid prescription, illicit drug use, or visible needle track marks
Found unresponsive or collapsed
signs: Classic triad: coma + miosis + respiratory depression
Cyanosis (central, from hypoventilation)
Bradycardia and hypotension
Hypothermia (prolonged collapse)
Needle track marks on arms, groin, or feet

bedside: Obs: RR <12, O2 sats (hypoxia), GCS (reduced)
BM — exclude hypoglycaemia as reversible cause of reduced consciousness
Urine drug screen — confirms opioid exposure
bloods: ABG: type 2 respiratory failure (↑CO2, ↓O2, ↓pH — respiratory acidosis)
FBC/U&E/CK — if prolonged collapse, CK elevated in rhabdomyolysis
Paracetamol and salicylate levels — routine in all overdose presentations
imaging: CXR: aspiration pneumonia if prolonged unconsciousness
CXR: non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (heroin-associated)

mx: IV or IM naloxone — opioid receptor antagonist; titrate dose to restore adequate respiratory rate (RR ≥12), not full consciousness
Repeated doses or IV infusion may be required — naloxone half-life (60–90 min) is shorter than most opioids
Supportive: airway positioning, high-flow O2, recovery position once responsive; admit for monitoring
traps: Naloxone half-life (60–90 min) is shorter than most opioids → patient can re-sedate after initial response; must observe for at least 4 hours or use infusion
Do not over-reverse — precipitates acute opioid withdrawal (agitation, vomiting, hypertension) and removes analgesia in pain patients
Always check BM first — hypoglycaemia mimics opioid toxidrome and is immediately reversible
Methadone and buprenorphine have very long half-lives — prolonged naloxone infusion almost always needed

name: Paracetamol overdose
presentation: Often asymptomatic in first 24h — deceptively well initially
Nausea and vomiting 0–24h post-ingestion
Right upper quadrant pain 24–72h (hepatocellular necrosis onset)
Jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy if severe hepatotoxicity (72–96h)
signs: RUQ tenderness (hepatic inflammation 24–72h)
Jaundice (hepatocellular failure >72h)
Signs of hepatic encephalopathy: confusion, asterixis, reduced GCS
Bruising or bleeding (coagulopathy — rising INR)

bedside: Paracetamol level at 4h post-ingestion (or earliest opportunity >4h) — plot on treatment nomogram
BM (hypoglycaemia sign of hepatic failure)
ECG if co-ingestants suspected
bloods: LFTs — ALT/AST peak at 72–96h, not day 1; early normal LFTs do not exclude developing hepatotoxicity
INR — key prognostic marker; rising INR indicates hepatic synthetic failure
U&E + creatinine — hepatorenal syndrome risk in severe cases
Venous blood gas — pH <7.3 after resuscitation = King's College criterion
imaging: USS liver + portal vasculature if hepatotoxicity confirmed — assess liver architecture and vascularity
Limited acute diagnostic role for imaging in early overdose

mx: Activated charcoal if presentation <1h post-ingestion and dose >150mg/kg (or >75mg/kg in high-risk)
IV N-acetylcysteine (NAC) — replenishes glutathione; start if paracetamol level plots above treatment line on nomogram, or if staggered/unknown timing (treat empirically)
King's College criteria for urgent liver transplant referral: pH <7.3 after resuscitation OR all three of INR >6.5 + creatinine >300 µmol/L + grade III–IV encephalopathy
traps: Staggered or unknown timing of ingestion — do NOT use nomogram; treat with NAC regardless of level
Patient appears well at 24h — early normalcy is classic trap; hepatotoxicity peaks 72–96h, not day 1
ALT/AST normal on presentation does not exclude serious overdose — repeat LFTs at 24h minimum
King's criteria: pH criterion alone is sufficient for referral; the triad (INR >6.5 + Cr >300 + grade III–IV encephalopathy) is the alternative — not all three needed if pH criterion met
